name: Geriatrics
description: An introductory course describing the needs of the elderly. The course will examine the changing population balance in Canada involving both medical and dental aspects of people over 60 years of age.

name: Practice Management
description: This course introduces the third-year dental students to practice management topics and concepts necessary for today's successful practice of dentistry. These topics include financial planning, banking, dental office records, different modes of practice, marketing, and time management. The emphasis

name: Clinical Practice II
description: A clinical course building on Clinical Practice I with emphasis on more complex patient needs involving all disciplines. Students perform oral surgery procedures as the prime operator. Students are also assigned to external programs such as the satellite clinics. Clinical observation may require . o

name: Special Registration
description: Dentistry undergraduate and postgraduate students who have been admitted to the University of Alberta Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry as a Visiting Student in accordance with the Faculty guidelines will be required to register in this course for the purpose of entitlement to registration in the Al

name: Introduction to DDS Advanced Placement
description: Students begin studying all phases of clinical dentistry including diagnosis and treatment planning, anaesthesia, periodontics, endodontics, operative dentistry, prosthodontics and orthodontics. Students receive an introduction to ethics in dentistry. Students perform tasks 2362 mainly in a pre-clin

name: Oral Pathology
description: A review of diseases of the oral and maxillofacial region and an exploration of recently acquired knowledge pertaining to them.

name: Oral Medicine I
description: Provides general pathophysiological and clinical foundations for Oral Medicine students. The content includes lectures, case- based seminars, and mentored clinical experiences. Special emphasis will be on the oral manifestations of systemic disease and on the dental management of medically-complex p

name: Oral Medicine Clinics I
description: Mentored clinical experience in the assessment and evidence-based management of oral lesions and oral manifestations of systemic diseases. Will include off-service experiences in relevant medical disciplines.

name: Orthodontic Techniques and Biomechanics
description: A comprehensive overview of the fundamentals of biomechanics in orthodontic treatment. Involves both the theoretical and practical application of biomechanical principles to orthodontic problem resolution through classroom and laboratory instruction.

name: Growth and Development
description: A detailed review of the postnatal growth and development of human craniofacial structures. Longitudinal and cross sectional growth data are presented. (Course offered in alternate years).

name: Orthodontic Seminars
description: Selected orthodontically related theoretical and practical topics along with orthodontic case management presentations are discussed in both seminar and preclinical formats.

name: Orthodontic Clinics
description: Applied clinical education and experience is obtained through supervised management of selected orthodontic cases.

name: Research Methodology
description: Review of scientific methodology and direction of students in the techniques of evaluating dental literature. A research proposal or literature review is required as part of this course.

name: Introduction to Research Methods and Data Management
description: This introductory course will prepare students to manage research studies and process data for analysis and interpretation. The course will include a collection of short didactic sessions that will be vodcasted, and interactivities both online and in-person. 2363

name: Special Topics in Oral Health Research
description: Content varies from year to year but is typically in the format of interactive discussions that are facilitated by experts in the area. Topics are announced prior to registration period. The student's transcript will carry a title descriptive of the content. May be repeated.

name: Introduction to Applied Statistics
description: Analysis of variance, multiple linear regressions, measures of association and agreement, logistic regression, and non- parametric methods. The concepts will be illustrated by problems in the dental and medical sciences. Applications to real data will be emphasized through the use of SPSS.

name: Applied Multivariate Statistical Analysis
description: Multivariate analysis of variance, repeated measures, multivariate linear regression, principal components, discriminate analysis, cluster analysis, and multidimensional scaling will be studied. The concepts will be illustrated by problems in dentistry and the medical sciences. Each student will sub

name: TMD/Orofacial Pain
description: Seminars in the pathophysiology, diagnosis and management of temporomandibular disorders and other orofacial pain disorders.

name: Temporomandibular Disorders Orofacial Pain Clinics
description: Applied clinical program in which clinical education and experience is obtained through supervised management of patients presenting with temporomandibular disorders and/or other orofacial pains.

name: Evidence Based Dentistry
description: This course focuses on the general principles of evidence based dentistry. It will cover some basic principles of epidemiology, formulation of the clinical question, search and acquisition of available scientific evidence, critical appraisal and application of evidence in a dentistry context. A fina

name: Systematic Reviews in Dentistry
description: This course focuses on the general principles of Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis in Dentistry. It will cover principles, procedures, problems and limitations in Systematic Reviews. Different types of Systematic Reviews would be analyzed. Use of Meta-Analysis as a statistical tool in Systematic R

name: Periodontology Fundamentals I
description: A comprehensive overview of the fundamentals of periodontal disease, etiology, diagnosis, treatment and maintenance. Involves both the theoretical and practical application of periodontal disease management and resolution.

name: Implantology Fundamentals I
description: A comprehensive overview of the fundamentals of implant dentistry. Involves both the theoretical and practical application of implant dentistry management as well as implant related bone and soft tissue manipulation.

name: Periodontology Seminars I
description: Selected periodontology related theoretical and practical topics along with periodontology case management presentations are discussed in both seminar and preclinical formats. 2364

name: Periodontology Clinics I
description: Applied clinical education and experience is obtained through supervised management of selected periodontology and implant cases.

name: Seminars in Oral Health Sciences
description: Seminars will focus on the major areas of research of Oral Health Sciences. Students must present one seminar on topics related to their field of research. Not required for MSc students in Oral Medicine and Orthodontics.

name: Advanced Oral Pathology
description: A microscope-based course to help the student understand clinicopathologic correlations in diagnosis and evidence-based management of oral disease. name: Oral Medicine Seminars II
description: Seminars (including Journal Club) focusing upon the assessment and evidence-based management of oral diseases or oral manifestations of systemic diseases.

name: Oral Medicine Clinics II
description: Mentored clinical experience in the assessment and evidence-based management of oral lesions or oral manifestations of systemic diseases. Will include off-service experiences in relevant medical disciplines.

name: Pharmacotherapeutics in Oral Medicine
description: A seminar series that will stress the pharmacological basis for selection and use of drugs as therapeutic agents in the management of oral diseases and orofacial pain disorders. Special emphasis will be placed on clinically significant drug interactions as they apply to the various groups of agents

description: Second year seminar and preclinical presentations. Requires successful completion of DENT 540.

name: Orthodontic Clinics
description: Second year applied clinical educational program. Requires successful completion of DENT 541.

name: Temporomandibular Disorders/Orofacial Pain Clinics
description: Second year applied clinical program in which clinical education and experience is obtained through supervised management of patients presenting with temporomandibular disorders and/or other orofacial pains.

description: Second year overview of the fundamentals of periodontal disease, etiology, diagnosis, treatment and maintenance. Involves both the theoretical and practical application of periodontal disease management and resolution.  2365

description: Second year comprehensive overview of the fundamentals of implant dentistry. Involves both the theoretical and practical application of implant dentistry management as well as implant related bone and soft tissue manipulation.

description: Second year seminar and preclinical presentations.

name: Periodontology Clinics II
description: Second year applied clinical educational program.

description: Overview of selected oral pathology topics pertinent to periodontal practice will be provided in lectures and seminars with emphasis on case-based discussions and pertinent literature review.

description: Seminars (including Journal Club) focusing upon the assessment and evidence-based management of oral diseases or oral manifestations of systemic diseases

name: Oral Medicine Clinics III
description: ★ 2.5 (fi 5) (two term, variable) Mentored clinical experience in the assessment and evidence-based management of oral lesions or oral manifestations of systemic diseases. Will include off-service experiences in relevant medical disciplines.

description: Third year seminar and preclinical presentations. Requires successful completion of DENT 640.

name: Orthodontic Clinics
description: Third year applied clinical educational program. There will be 3 one-week internships as part of this course. Requires successful completion of DENT 641.

name: Temporomandibular Disorders/Orofacial Pain Clinics III
description: ★ 2.5 (fi 5) (two term, variable) Third year applied clinical program in which clinical education and experience is obtained through supervised management of patients presenting with temporomandibular disorders and/or other orofacial pains.

name: Periodontology Fundamentals III
description: Third year case discussions based on fundamentals of periodontal disease, etiology, diagnosis, treatment and maintenance.

name: Foundations of Medicine and Dentistry
description: Focus on integrating basic principles of medical and biological sciences as the foundation for the Medicine and Dentistry curriculum. Open only to students registered in the MD or DDS program.

name: Endocrine System
description: An examination of the endocrine system in health and disease, with particular reference to the mechanisms of disturbances in the endocrine system, and the management of these conditions. Open only to students registered in the MD or DDS program.

name: Cardiovascular System
description: The normal function of the heart and blood vessels, the changes in these functions which occur in disease and the management of the conditions which result from such changes in function. Open only to students registered in the MD or DDS program.

name: Pulmonary System
description: The normal function of the lungs, the changes in these functions which occur in disease and the management of the conditions which result from such changes in function. Open only to students registered in the MD or DDS program.

name: Renal System
description: The normal function of the kidney, the changes in these functions which occur in disease and the management of the conditions which result from such changes in function. name: Advanced Topics in Earth Observation Science
description: Advanced treatment of methods and applications in earth observation science. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas523 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic is repeated. Topics include: (1) Multi- and hyperspectral remote sensing; (2) Radar remote sensing; (3) Geoin

name: Mineralogy - Petrology - Geochemistry
description: Studies in geochemistry, petrology and mineralogy. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas536 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic is repeated. Topics include: (1) Seminar; (2) Thermodynamics; (3) Mantle Studies. [Faculty of Science]

name: Isotope Geology: Radioactive Systems
description: Theory and systematics of radioactive decay, geochronology and isotopic tracing U-Pb, Rb-Sr, Sm-Nd, Re-Os and other radioisotope systems. Applications of natural radioactive isotope variation to a variety of problems spanning low and high temperature geologic processes. [Faculty of Science]

name: Isotope Geology: Stable Isotope
description: Theory of light-element isotope fractionation; isotope variations in the meteoric cycle, igneous, metamorphic, sedimentary rocks and ore deposits. Isotope techniques in paleothermometry and paleoclimate studies. Isotope biogeochemistry, oil and gas. [Faculty of Science]

name: Hydrogeology
description: The storage and movement of water through Earth media. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas544 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic is repeated. Topics include: (1) Quantitative Hydrogeology; (2) Regional Groundwater Flow. [Faculty of Science]

name: Methods and Instrumentation in Geology
description: Course will cover analytical techniques such as probe. SEM, XRD, TIMS/gas source mass spectrometry, superpress, XRF, ICP-MS, TEM, NMR, SHRIMP and microthermometric techniques. [Faculty of Science] 2391

name: Topics in Geomorphology and Sedimentology
description: Selected, contemporary theories of landscape and sediment formation in glacial, glaciofluvial, alluvial, and periglacial environments. [Faculty of Science]

name: Advanced Geobiology
description: The relationship between biology and geology. Ichnology, geomicrobiology, and microfossil content. The evolution of animal-rock relationships through time. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas560 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 460 or 560 is repeated.

name: Advanced Petrology: Diamond Exploration
description: Concepts in mantle petrology, geochemistry, volcanology, and diamond research that form the basis of modern exploration strategies for kimberlites and diamonds. Classes concurrent with EAS 461. [Faculty of Science]

name: Advanced Stratigraphy and Sedimentary Basins
description: The science of rock strata in a sequence stratigraphic framework; sequence stratigraphic models; classification and evolution of sedimentary basins; applications of sequence stratigraphy to depositional systems and tectonic settings. Classes concurrent with EAS 462. Not available to students with cr

name: Advanced Applied Hydrocarbon Geoscience
description: Advanced topics in the characterization of petroleum resources and the regional occurrence of hydrocarbons. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas564 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 464 or 564 is repeated. Topics include: (1) Regional and Petroleum Hydr

name: Advanced Sedimentology
description: The science of sedimentary rocks, focusing on the interpretation of sedimentary strata. Topics vary: visit the Earth and Atmospheric Sciences course listing website for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 565 or EAS 465 is repeated. Topics include: (1) Carbonate

name: Advanced Petrogenesis
description: Origin and formation of igneous and metamorphic rocks in the light of field, mineralogical, chemical and experimental evidence. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas566 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 466 or 566 is repeated. Topics include: (1) Cratons

name: Advanced Planetary Geology
description: The geologically evolving Earth and its context in an evolving solar system. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas567 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 467 or EAS 567 is repeated. Topics include: (1) Planetary Systems; (2) Earth System Evolution (Not ava

name: Advanced Geochemical Processes
description: Application of geochemistry to Earth materials and geological settings. Topics vary: see www.eas.ualberta.ca/eas568 for details. May be taken more than once for credit provided no topic in EAS 468 or 568 is repeated. Topics include: (1) Geochemistry of Ore Deposits (Not available to students with cr

name: Advanced Climatology
description: A study of recent developments in climatology. Climate models and their use in examining past and future climates. Interactions between the atmosphere and terrestrial systems. [Faculty of Science]

name: Advanced Contaminant Hydrogeology
description: An introduction to principles of groundwater chemistry, the chemical evolution of natural groundwater flow systems, sources of contamination, and mass transport processes. Hydrogeologic aspects of waste disposal and groundwater remediation. Research project. Classes concurrent with EAS 425. Not avai

name: Advanced Clouds and Storms
description: Cloud properties; convection; precipitation; weather radar; severe convective storms; precipitation forecasting. Research project. Classes concurrent with EAS 470. Not available to students with credit in EAS 470.

name: Advanced Digital Remote Sensing
description: Introduces the interactions of electromagnetic radiation with terrestrial materials (rocks, soils, water, snow). These notions are fundamental for the interpretation of optical, thermal, and radar remote sensing imagery. Labs focus on image processing with emphasis on radiometric and geometric enhan

name: Advanced Atmosphere-Ocean Dynamics
description: Synoptic-scale processes; the general circulation; turbulence; oceanic mixing; wind-driven circulation; waves in the atmosphere and ocean; baroclinic instability; tides. Class concurrent with EAS 475. Not available to students with credit in EAS 475. [Faculty of Science] East Asian Studies: Undergra

name: Understanding East Asia
description: Important aspects of pre-modern and modern East Asia from a broad interdisciplinary perspective.

name: Overview of the Chinese Language System
description: Discussion of basic features of the Chinese language. Designed to be taken concurrently with CHINA 201 or 202. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 208.

name: Language in Chinese Society
description: Topics may include language and gender, identity, dialects. Lectures in English. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 241.

name: Overview of the Japanese Language
description: To be taken concurrently with JAPAN 201 or 202. Note: Not open to students with credit in or completing JAPAN 301. Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 241. 2393

name: Linguistics in Japanese Everyday Life
description: Theoretical and practical approaches to Japanese in real-life communicative situations.

name: East Asian Religions
description: Survey of the major religious traditions of China, Japan, and Korea.

name: Interculturalism and East Asian Music
description: Survey of the major music traditions of China, Japan, and Korea through an exploration of Silk Road influences on Chinese music and Chinese musical influences on Japanese and Korean Music.

name: Overview of Chinese Culture
description: Major trends in Chinese literature, art, and other forms of cultural expression, from earliest times to the modern period. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 240.

name: The Chinese Literati
description: Literary and historical perspectives on the elite of pre-modern China.

name: Modernity and Contemporary Chinese Civilization
description: The development of modernity in modern and contemporary China, Taiwan, and/or Hong Kong and its influence on literature, cinema, visual culture and/or popular culture.

name: Daoism and Chinese Civilization
description: Ancient Chinese Daoist thought and its influence on the later history and culture of China.

name: Overview of Japanese Culture
description: Major trends in Japanese literature, art, and other forms of cultural expression, from earliest times to the modern period. Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 240.

name: Samurai, Geisha, and Ghosts
description: Literature and art of early modern Japan, with attention paid to representations of warriors, women, and the supernatural.

name: Overview of Korean Culture
description: Major trends in Korean literature, art, and other forms of cultural expression, from earliest times to the modern period. Note: Not open to students with credit in KOREA 240.

name: Introduction to Chinese Linguistics
description: Sound system, basic sentence structure, writing system, and language change and variation. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 308.

name: Chinese Psycholinguistics
description: Issues and methods in Chinese language processing and spoken language comprehension.  2394

name: Introduction to Japanese Linguistics
description: Sound system, parts of speech, basic sentence structure, writing system, and language change and variation. Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 325.

name: Japanese Sociolinguistics
description: Introduction to social and interactional aspects of the Japanese language. Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 326.

name: Topics in East Asian Religions
description: Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Pre-modern Chinese Literature in Translation
description: From earliest times through the Qing Dynasty. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 321.

name: Modern Chinese Literature in Translation
description: Chinese literature of the 20th and 21st centuries. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 322.

name: Modern Japanese Literature in Translation
description: Selected works by prominent writers from 1868 to the present. Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 322.

name: The Japanese Supernatural
description: Representations of the supernatural in Japanese literature from the earliest times to the present day.

name: Culture and Identity in Taiwan
description: The relationship between culture and identity in Taiwan through the study of literature and film in translation. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 351.

name: Pre-Modern Korean Literature in Translation
description: From earliest times through the Joseon (Choson) Dynasty.

name: Introduction to Korean Linguistics
description: Sound system, parts of speech, basic sentence structure, writing system, and language change and variation.  Note: Not open to students with credit in KOREA 325.

name: Korean Sociolinguistics
description: Introduction to social and cultural aspects of the Korean language.  2395

name: Chinese Linguistics
description: Discussion of the major linguistic features of the Chinese language. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 408.

name: Japanese Linguistics
description: Discussion of the major linguistic features of the Japanese language. Lectures in English. Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 425.

name: Orientalisms and Occidentalisms
description: Critical reading of Western representations of the East, and Eastern representations of the West. All readings in English.  Not to be taken by students with credit in C LIT 426.

name: Advanced Studies in Japanese Religions
description: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Topics in East/West Critical Theory
description: Readings in English of East Asian and Euro-American philosophers and critics.

name: Colonial and Post Colonial Culture in East Asia
description: Colonialism, soft power and transnational connections in East Asia through the lens of cultural production.

name: Topics in East Asian Studies
description: May be repeated for credit when course content differs. Prerequisite EASIA 101 and *3 in EASIA at a senior level, or consent of Department.

name: Classical Chinese Poetry
description: Emphasis on the production of poetry as a cultural object. Note: Not open to students with credit in CHINA 410.  CHINA 341 recommended.

name: Readings in Classical Chinese Fiction
description: Analytical readings of premodern fiction written in classical and/or early vernacular Chinese. May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Topics in Chinese Literature and Film
description: A crossdisciplinary study of Chinese and Sinophone literary and cinematic texts in English translation. May be repeated for credit when content varies.

name: Topics in Chinese Studies
description: May be repeated for credit when course content differs. May involve discussions in Chinese.  2396

name: Topics in Japanese Literary History
description: The major works of a particular period or aspect of Japanese literature. Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Japanese Theatre from the Noh to the Avant-garde
description: Note: Not open to students with credit in JAPAN 416.

name: Topics in Japanese Studies
description: May be repeated for credit when course content differs. Not open to web registration.

name: Topics in Taiwan Studies
description: Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Topics in Taiwan Literature
description: Readings in Taiwan literature with emphasis on tradition, theme, and technique. Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Languages and Cultures of the Ryukyus
description: Introduction to linguistic and socio-cultural aspects of the Ryukyus, a past independent kingdom with strong ties to China, more recently subsumed by Japan.

name: Topics in Korean Studies
description: Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Topics in Korean Literary History
description: The major works of a particular period or aspect of Korean literature. Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Readings in Classical Korean Fiction
description: Analytical readings of premodern Korean fiction. Note: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Honors Seminar
description: Note: Open to fourth year Honors students only. East Asian Studies: Graduate

name: Japanese Linguistics
description: Discussion of the major linguistic features of the Japanese language. Lectures in English.  2397

name: Topics in East/West Critical Theory
description: Readings of East Asian and Euro-American philosophers and critics.

name: Languages and Cultures of the Ryukyus
description: Introduction to linguistic and socio-cultural aspects of the Ryukyus, a past independent kingdom with strong ties to China, more recently subsumed by Japan.

name: East Asian Language Pedagogy
description: Discussion and application of the theory and practice of teaching East Asian languages. Lectures in English.

name: Topics in East Asian Research
description: An inquiry into the diversity of disciplines used in the study of East Asian literatures and cultures.

name: Directed Reading in East Asian Studies
description: May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Literary and Cultural Theory
description: Literary and cultural theory in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.  Note: This course is equivalent to MLCS 652. Economics: Undergraduate Department of Economics Faculty of Arts Notes 1. See also INT D 257, INT D 302, INT D 303, INT D 346, and INT D 369 for courses which are offered by more t

name: Introduction to Microeconomics
description: How markets and governments determine which products are produced and how income is distributed in the Canadian economy. Not open to students with credit in ECON 204.

name: Introduction to Macroeconomics
description: Employment, inflation, international payments, monetary policy, and fiscal policy, all in the Canadian economy.  Not open to students with credit in ECON 204.

name: An Introduction to Writing for Economics Students
description: Formal instruction in the basic principles of writing, including a review of basic grammar. The focus is on hands-on practice in order to develop clear writing skills. Examples and exercises will be drawn from economics. Students will also make oral presentations. Prerequisite or corequisite: ECON 1

name: Selected Topics in Economics I
description: Content varies from year to year. Topics announced prior to registration period.  Additional prerequisites may be required; consult the Department for further information.

name: Principles of Economics
description: An introduction to economic principles as applied to business organization and finance; price determination; enterprise costs and output optimization; commercial and central banking; national income analysis. For students enrolled in the Faculty of Engineering only. Not open to students with credit

name: Chinese Economic Development
description: A survey of the characteristics of and recent developments in the Chinese economy emphasizing the nature and consequences of China's economic reforms and Canada's economic relations with China.

name: An Introduction to the Economics of Developing Countries
description: A survey of the major approaches to and problems of economic development in the less developed countries with particular emphasis on issues relating to savings and investment, income distribution, employment and population growth, and trade and aid.

name: Technology, Institutions and Economic Growth
description: Differences in technology and institutions are used to explain why some countries are richer than others; why economic growth rates differ across time and jurisdictions; and causes of convergence/divergence in cross-country growth rates.

name: History of Economic Thought
description: The development of economic thought in social and political context. Major schools of thought from Greek philosophers up to the Marxist, Classical, and Neoclassical doctrines.

name: Economics of the Environment
description: Economic growth and the deterioration of the environment; types, causes, theory, policy, and measurement, and current Canadian environmental topics.  Not open to students with credit in ECON 369, INT D 369, or INT D 225 offered as Economics and the Environment.

name: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory I
description: The theory of consumer behavior; theory of production and cost; price and output determination under competition, monopoly and other market structures.

name: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory I
description: Models of price, interest rate, output, and employment determination; the impact of fiscal, monetary, and supply shocks; open economy macroeconomics with fixed and flexible exchange rates, and prices as well as international capital mobility.

name: Quantitative Methods in Economics
description: Introduction to the use of statistical methods in economics with computer applications.  Note: Designed for students taking Economics as a major subject of concentration. Department permission must be obtained by other students wishing to take this course. ECON 299 or equivalent must be taken before

name: International Economics
description: A survey of the principles of international economics and the applications to economic policy. Topics include international trade in goods and financial assets, trade policy and exchange rate determination. Note: Not open to students with credit in or enrolled in ECON 421 or 422.

name: Labor Economics
description: Theory and empirical evidence concerning the supply of and demand for labor services, wage differentials, and the impact of unions, with particular reference to Canadian contemporary issues. Some of the policy issues to be discussed are income maintenance, unemployment insurance, and minimum wage le

name: Money and Banking
description: Financial intermediation, commercial banking, central banking, securities markets, and regulation of the banking and financial sectors, the money supply process and monetary control.  Students may not receive credit for both ECON 341 and FIN 414.

name: The Economics of Public Expenditures
description: Analysis of public sector expenditures in Canada. The rationale for government spending and the problems in the provision of public services.

name: Taxation Policy and Structure
description: Analysis of the Canadian tax structure and its role in attaining certain goals of society; requirements for an optimal tax structure. Prerequisite or corequisite: ECON 281 or consent of Department.

name: Health Economics
description: Resource allocation and public policy in health care, including determinants of health status, market structures, incentives and the effects of imperfect information.

name: Behavioural and Experimental Economics
description: Introduction to experimental methods in economics, with focus on laboratory studies of economic behaviour.

name: Resource Economics
description: Issues in the production of exhaustible and renewable natural resources, including exploration, extraction, and taxation; scarcity and pricing; contemporary Canadian resource policy issues.  Students may not receive credit for both ECON 365 and AREC 365.

name: Energy Economics
description: The economics of producing and consuming energy: pricing, role in economic growth; energy sources and markets; the role of government; regulation and other energy policy issues.

name: Industrial Organization
description: A survey of the behavior and performance of firms in different market structures and discussion of public policy toward the different structures. Note: Not open to students with credit in ECON 471 or 472.  2400

name: Law and Economics: Common Law and Economic Incentives
description: Economic implications of common law: property, contract, and tort; economic logic underlying different doctrines within the law, and illustrations of the law as an economic institution; externality, risk and deterrence, and other leading issues.

name: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory II
description: Designed for majors and Honors students in Economics. Extensions and applications of microeconomic theory: intertemporal choice, risk, uncertainty and expected utility; oligopoly and game theory; externalities, public goods, adverse selection, moral hazard, and asymmetric information; general equili

name: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory II
description: Designed for majors and Honors students in Economics. Theories of stabilization policy; expectations; the government budget constraint; inflation and unemployment; business cycles and growth; theories of aggregate consumption, investment, money demand, and money supply.

name: Applications of Mathematics to Economics I
description: Elements of logic and set theory, linear algebra, differential calculus and their conjunction, as used in classical and modern economic analysis.

name: Applications of Mathematics to Economics II
description: Difference and differential equations, linear inequalities, convexity, programming; assorted theorems of special use in modern economic analysis.

name: Introductory Econometrics
description: An elementary treatment of the major topics in econometrics with emphasis on applied regression methods.  Note: Not open to students with credit in AREC 313.

name: Honors Essay: Fourth-Year Honors Economics
description: Required for fourth-year honors students choosing the honors essay route.  Only open to students registered in the Economics Honors Essay Route. Credit will not be granted for both ECON 497 and ECON 400.

name: Pacific Rim Economic Development
description: Analyzes the role of particular markets and institutions in selected Pacific Rim economies. Special emphasis is given to either China or Japan; students should consult the Department of Economics to find which country is being emphasized in a given year.

name: Economics of Developing Countries
description: An introduction to models of growth and development; the role of agriculture, industry, finance, and trade in structural transformation of developing countries; approaches to development planning.

name: International Trade
description: Nature and relevance of international trade; early trade doctrines; the theory of comparative advantage, classical and modern approaches and empirical evidence for them; new approaches to the pure theory of international trade; economic growth and international trade; market imperfections and trade;

name: International Payments
description: Types of international transactions, macroeconomics in an open economy, exchange rates, balance of payments adjustments, and other issues in the international monetary system.

name: Topics in International Trade and Development in India
description: International trade and economic development from the perspective of India. Emphasis given to trade policy, poverty, inequality, productivity, and labor markets in India.

name: Labor Economics
description: Topics include demand for labor, supply of labor, wage differentials, trade union behavior, the minimum wage, education and income distribution, discrimination, mandatory retirement, and non-market work.

name: Economics of Education
description: The analysis of education from the perspective of quantitative and theoretical economics, with a focus on human capital development from preschool to university, the effect of education on the economy, and education policy.

name: The Economics of Financial Markets
description: The measurement of risk; portfolio analysis; hedging and speculation; market microstructure; asset pricing and market equilibrium.  Students may not receive credit for both ECON 442 and FIN 412.

name: Topics in Public Economics
description: Possibilities and limitations of efficiency and equity of markets and government intervention.

name: Urban Economics
description: Urban spatial structure, residential land use, firm location decisions, housing, transportation, and urban public finance.

name: Environmental and Natural Resource Policy
description: Environmental and natural resource law; domestic and global policy issues related to renewable and non-renewable resources.  Not open to students with credit in ECON 466 or ENCS 473.

name: Strategic Behavior of the Firm
description: Oligopoly theory, cartel formation, product differentiation and advertising, entry into markets and strategic entry deterrence, and research and development.  2402

name: Market Power: Theory and Policy
description: Market definition and measurement of market power. Canadian competition policy, including merger, predation, abuse of dominance, price discrimination, tie-in sales, exclusive dealing, resale price maintenance, collusion and bid rigging.

name: Advanced Microeconomic Theory
description: Consumer and producer theory, and selected topics.

name: Advanced Macroeconomic Theory
description: Business cycle theory, microfoundations of macro models, government budget constraints, expectations formation, the open economy, and representative agent optimizing models.

name: Game Theory and Economic Applications
description: Analysis of structure and equilibrium of games. Applications to economic problems such as bargaining, auctions and collusion.

name: Macroeconomic Policy
description: Analysis of the objectives, instruments and methods of macroeconomics policy using contemporary macroeconomics theory and examples from the policy experience of Canada and other nations.

name: Economic Forecasting
description: Statistical methods for modeling and forecasting trends, seasonal, and cyclical components; ARMA models; regression models; forecast evaluation; and forecasting in the presence of unit roots.

name: Econometric Methods
description: Econometric problems and techniques with emphasis on regression methods and hypothesis testing. Single equation techniques and introduction to simultaneous equation systems. Matrix algebra is used extensively.  Prerequisite or  Not open to students with credit for ECON 408. Credit will not be grante

name: Microeconomic Theory I
description: Producer and consumer behavior; partial equilibrium models of perfectly and imperfectly competitive markets; Walrasian general equilibrium; welfare economics.

name: Microeconomic Theory II
description: Choice under uncertainty; contingent claims and models of general equilibrium under uncertainty; markets with information asymmetries; non-cooperative game theory, games of incomplete information, repeated games, and bargaining theory.

name: Time Series Methods in Financial Econometrics
description: Topics may include ARIMA modelling, spectral analysis, state-space models and the Kalman filter, nonstationary analysis, vector autoregressions, conditional heteroskedasticity and nonlinear models.  2403

name: Economic Development I
description: The techniques of development planning; qualitative and quantitative problems associated with the drafting and implementation of plans and programs; assessment of internal and external resources available for development and problems of measurement and mobilization of resources.

name: Economic Development II
description: Economic policy alternatives in a context of growth and development; problems of inflation, balance of payments, disequilibrium, concentration of growth effects; the role of international aid and other external measures.

name: Labor Economics I
description: Factors influencing the supply of, and demand for, labor services and the process of relative wage determination in the long and short run. Determination of money wage levels, aggregate labor-force participation, and the level and structure of aggregate employment and unemployment.

name: Economics of Financial Markets
description: The course surveys Asset Pricing Theory with an emphasis on the utility-based discount-factor approach. The discount factor provides a unifying framework for the evaluation of most classes of assets including stocks, bonds, and derivatives. In particular, the course reviews mean- variance analysis,

name: Public Expenditure
description: The theory of the role of the public sector in a market economy; market failures, income redistribution, public choice, and fiscal federalism.

name: Economics of Taxation
description: Effects of taxes on allocation, distribution and stabilization objectives. Evaluation of major taxes with particular attention paid to efficiency and incidence considerations.

name: Health Economics
description: Theoretical and applied issues in the determination of health models and a survey of contemporary health economic policy issues.

name: Environmental Economics
description: Economic theory and policy relating to environmental problems; welfare and public policy issues in environmental decision making. Environmental law; transboundary pollution; economic instruments for pollution control.

name: The Economics of Exhaustible Resources
description: Theoretical, empirical, and policy studies in the following areas: supply and pricing under various market structures, the demand for exhaustible resources, exploration, resource extraction under price and technological uncertainty, taxation of exhaustible resources, exhaustible resources and the ma

name: Strategic Behavior of the Firm
description: Game theory; oligopoly theory; dynamic price competition; cartel formation; product differentiation; and advertising; entry and strategic entry deterrence; research and development.

name: Market Power: Theory and Policy
description: Market definition and measurement of market power. Principles of competition policy, including merger, predation, abuse of dominance, price discrimination, vertical market restrictions, collusion and bid rigging.

name: The Economics of Regulation: Theory and Applications
description: Economic foundations of regulation. This course covers problems of natural monopoly, incentive regulation, deregulation, environmental regulation, insurance and financial regulation, universal service obligation, network access pricing, and product quality regulation.

name: Macroeconomic Theory I
description: An examination of the core topics in macroeconomic theory. These will generally include methods of modelling output, employment, prices, business cycles, and macroeconomic policy. Prerequisite or corequisite: ECON 481 and 482 or equivalent.

name: Macroeconomic Theory II
description: This course extends the analysis of ECON 581 and introduces students to more advanced issues.

name: Graduate Research Workshop I
description: The course will aid students in identifying and developing a research topic, and develop skills in the writing and presentation of research. Students will be expected to present, write critiques of, and participate in discussions of published articles or working papers.

name: Econometric Theory and Applications
description: Advanced treatment of estimation, inference and econometric problems and techniques, including the use of matrix operations and statistical distribution theory, with an emphasis on applied econometric analysis.  Note: Not open to students with credit in ECON 506.

name: Applied Econometrics
description: The role of economic theory in the process of specification and estimation of models. Interpretation and critical evaluation of applied work by means of selected topics in economics and econometrics.

name: Directed Research Project
description: Economie: Cours de 1er cycle Faculté Saint-Jean

name: Introduction à la micro-économie
description: Analyse du processus de détermination des produits et des quantités à produire en économie de marché. Étude de la répartition du revenu au Canada.

name: Introduction à la macro-économie
description: Étude de l'emploi, de l'inflation, des politiques monétaires et fiscales au Canada. Préalable: ECONE 101.

name: Enjeux économiques mondiaux
description: Description de la place du Canada dans le monde économique. Analyses des principales forces économiques au niveau mondial. Discussion de ce que constitue le libre-échange. Identification des blocs économiques (ALENA, Mercosur, UE, ASEAN).

name: Microéconomie intermédiaire I
description: Présentation de la théorie néoclassique du consommateur, du producteur et de la détermination des prix et des quantités dans le cas de marchés concurrentiels, de monopole et de certaines autres structures de marché. Préalable(s): ECONE 101 ou équivalent. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudi

name: Macroéconomie intermédiaire I
description: Introduction aux modèles analytiques de la macroéconomie. Présentation des modèles à prix fixes et flexibles de la détermination du taux d'intérêt, de la production et de l'emploi. Étude des relations entre le marché de l'emploi et l'offre agrégée. Analyse des conséquences de la politique fiscale et

name: Méthodes quantitatives en économie
description: Introduction à l'utilisation des outils mathématiques en économie avec applications. Préalable(s): ECONE 101 et 102, STATQ 151 ou STAT 141 ou SCI 151 et MATHQ 114 (ou 113) ou SCI 100. Note: Ce cours doit être suivi avant ECONE 399.

name: Thèmes d'économie I
description: Le contenu peut varier d'année en année. Les sujets seront annoncés avant la période d'inscription. Préalable: ECONE 101. Des préalables additionnels pourront être exigés.

name: Économie internationale
description: Aperçu des principes de l'économie internationale. Application de ces principes à la politique économique. L'accent est mis sur des sujets comme le commerce international, l'Investissement étranger et la balance des paiements. Préalable(s): ECONE 101 et 102, ou ECONE 223. 2406

name: Économie des ressources
description: Étude des problèmes reliés à l'exploitation de ressources naturelles renouvelables et non-renouvelables, incluant l'exploration, l'extraction et la taxation; rareté et détermination des prix des ressources; politiques canadiennes actuelles touchant ces sujets. Préalable(s): ECONE 101 ou équivalent.

name: Microéconomie intermédiaire II
description: Développement élaboré de la théorie microéconomique et de certaines applications, avec un accent sur l'équilibre général. Certains des sujets suivants seront aussi abordés : Choix intertemporels, choix risqués, l'incertain et l'hypothèse de l'utilité attendue; l'oligopole et la théorie des jeux, mod

name: Macroéconomie intermédiare II
description: Théories des politiques de stabilisation; anticipation; les contraintes budgétaires du gouvernement; inflation et chômage; cycles économiques; théories de la consommation agrégées, de l'investissement, de la demande de monnaie et de l'offre de monnaie. Préalable(s) : ECONE 281, 282 et 299 ou équival

name: Introduction à l'économétrie
description: Introduction aux méthodes statistiques utilisées dans l'analyse des problèmes économiques empiriques. Note: Sections reserved for students in the TYP Program include a 3 hour seminar component in addition to the 3 hour lecture componen

name: English for Engineering Students
description: This course aims to develop the student's ability to provide effective written and oral information. It will focus on instruction in fundamental writing skills, including building effective sentences and paragraphs, and on learning to communicate clearly across a range of genres and media used in ac

name: History of the Book
description: An introduction to the social and cultural history of material text, and to the critical concepts and methods key to its study, that emphasizes the relationship between the production of books and the production of culture.

name: Histories of Reading
description: An introduction to the social and cultural histories of reading, and to the critical concepts and methods key to its study, that emphasizes the relationship between reading and the production of culture.

name: Reading Histories in Texts
description: An introduction to the critical concepts and methods for reading literary texts historically that emphasizes the relationship between representation and history.

name: Introduction to the English Language
description: Introduces the grammar of English sounds, words, and sentences as a basis for further studies in language and literature.

name: Signs and Texts
description: An introduction to theories of signification and textuality, and to the issues and debates surrounding the relationship between language systems and the production of meanings, as they bear on literary analysis.

name: Theories of Reading and Interpretation
description: An introduction to theories of reading and interpretation, and to the issues and debates surrounding the relationship between literary events and the reception of meanings, as they bear on literary analysis.  2479

name: Narrative Theory and Poetics
description: An introduction to narratology and poetics, as well as to the practices of close reading and the formalist analysis of literary texts, as they bear on literary analysis.

name: Reading Gender and Sexuality
description: An introduction to dynamics of gender and sexuality in literary and other cultural texts, and to the critical concepts and methods key to their study.

name: Reading Class and Ideology
description: An introduction to dynamics of class and ideology in literary and other cultural texts, and to the critical concepts and methods key to their study.

name: Reading Race and Ethnicity
description: An introduction to dynamics of race and ethnicity in literary and other cultural texts, and to the critical concepts and methods key to their study.

name: Reading Empire and the Postcolonial
description: An introduction to dynamics of colonization and its resistances in literary and other cultural texts, and to the critical concepts and methods key to their study.

name: The Literary Institution
description: An introduction to theories of the literary institution and to the issues and debates surrounding literary criticism as a social and political practice that takes place within the horizon of history and under certain systemic constraints.

name: Essay Writing for Education Students
description: This course, designed to increase the student's ability to write effective essays, emphasizes the study of grammar, punctuation, and sentence and paragraph structure. The study of models of prose style is integrated with frequent practice in writing. ENGL 299 is not a remedial course. Note: Restrict

name: Social and Cultural History of the English Language
description: Studies in the historical development of the English Language.  Note: not to be taken by students with credit in former ENGL 311.

name: Social and Cultural History of Genre
description: Studies in the theory and practice of genre. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Literary and Cultural Theories
description: Studies in critical and theoretical currents within literary studies. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Literature and Religion
description: Studies in selected texts, movements, and traditions that reflect the interaction of religion with literature and culture. Content and period focus may vary.  2480

name: Indigenous Literatures: Intellectual Traditions
description: Studies of the contributions of Indigenous writers to the formation of their intellectual and community traditions. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Indigenous Literatures: Literary Movements
description: Studies in the literary and cultural currents within Indigenous writing. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Postcolonial Literature and Culture: African Writing in English

description: Selected works from the Irish context. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Postcolonial Literature and Culture: Indian Writing in English
description: Selected works from the Indian context. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Postcolonial Literature and Culture: Middle-Eastern Writing in English
description: Selected works from the Middle-Eastern context. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Old English Language and Literature

name: Medieval Literature and Culture: Medieval and Tudor Drama

description: Selected works from the American context, 1945 to the present.

name: Early 20th-Century British Literature and Culture
description: Selected works from the British context to mid-century.  Note: not to be taken by students with credit in ENGL 370.

name: Contemporary Literature and Culture

name: Early 20th-Century Literature and Culture: Drama

name: Canadian Literature and Culture: Early 20th-Century Texts
description: Selected works from the Canadian context to mid-century.  Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in ENGL 372.

name: Canadian Literature and Culture: Reading Canadian Cultures
description: Studies in the cultural politics of representation in Canadian texts. Content and period focus may vary.

name: Canadian Literature and Culture: Late 20th-Century Texts
description: Selected works from the Canadian context since mid-century.  Note: not to taken by students with credit in ENGL 372. 2483

name: Canadian Literature and Culture: Contemporary Cultural Texts

name: Introduction to Film Study
description: Introduction to basic formal concepts in film analysis including mise-en-scène, cinematographic properties, editing, and sound, as well as narrative qualities.

name: Introduction to Film History I
description: A survey of world cinema from 1890 to 1950, with emphasis on major historical developments and important individual films. Prerequisite or corequisite: FS 100. Not to be taken by students with credit in FS 200.

name: Introduction to Film History II
description: A survey of world cinema from 1950 to present, with emphasis on major historical developments and important individual films.  Prerequisite or corequisite: FS 201. Not to be taken by students with credit in FS 200.

name: Television from Broadcasting to Screen Cultures
description: The socio-cultural role of TV from the network to multi-platform eras with an emphasis on theories of power and representation. Prerequisite/

name: Introduction to Film Theory
description: General survey of major currents and debates in film theory, including early theories on the ontology of the film image, semiotic approaches to film as language, Marxist and psychoanalytic concepts of spectatorship and the film image, the intersections of film and ideology, and the phenomenological

name: Quebec Film
description: History and aesthetic developments in Quebec film, from 1930s to present.

name: English-Canadian Film
description: Major trends in English Canadian film, such as documentary, feature film, animation, and experimental film.  2497

name: Transnational Crime Cinema
description: Examining the ways in which crime and criminality have been represented in narrative cinema across cultures and nationalities.

name: The Western Film
description: Survey of the cinematic Western from the silent period to the present, with emphasis on the decades between 1930 and 1970.

name: Science Fiction Film
description: A study of the Science Fiction film genre as an imaginative displacement of social and cultural concerns that define the context in which the films emerge.

name: Film Noir
description: A study of the stylistic, thematic, and ideological features of the American film genre known as film noir.

name: The French New Wave
description: Examines the aesthetic, cultural and political principles animating the films of the 1960s cinematic movement known as the French New Wave.  Not to be taken by students with credit in FS 362.

name: Animation
description: An overview of the development of the animated film in a global context. Production practices may include pin screen, cell, clay, collage, stop-motion, and computer animation.

name: Gender and Sexuality in Film
description: Intersections between film and cultural notions and practices of gender and sexuality.

name: Screening Comedy
description: Examines the history of comedy across cinema, television, and digital platforms, drawing on theories of humor and laughter.

name: Monsters, Slashers and Ghosts
description: Examines the horror genre from the silent era to the present.

name: Experimental Film
description: Avant-garde, abstract and structural film. The history and changing conceptions of experimental film, with examples from the silent era to the present.

name: Making Television: Production Cultures
description: Examines the cultural and industrial dimensions of televisual production and distribution into the post-network era.

name: Television Genres
description: Analysis of specific genres within their cultural, historical, and industrial contexts.  2498

name: Central and East-European Film
description: Historical and aesthetic developments in the cinemas of the Czech Republic, Poland, and Hungary from the early years of cinema to the post-1989 period.

name: East Asian Cinema
description: Survey of East Asian cinemas such as mainland China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Japan, and South Korea.

name: Contemporary Hollywood
description: Concentrates on commercial American filmmaking since the 1960s. Special attention will be given to defining Postmodernism and to historically situating its rise within the development of American cinema.

name: Screening Race
description: Examines representations of race, ethnicity, and identity on screen with an emphasis on critical race theories.

name: Film and Technology
description: An examination of how technology influences the patterns of film production.

name: Topics in Filmmakers
description: Concentrated study of the works of individual filmmakers. The course will deal with one to three important filmmakers through representative films.

name: Topics in Film Studies
description: A seminar-based examination of specialized topics in film.

name: Global Television and Screen Cultures
description: Transnational flows of popular entertainment and their relationship to local and global cultural identities.

name: Analyzing Television
description: Critical studies of televisual narratives, ideologies, discourses, and audience reception practices.

Les étudiants mesureront et géreront un portefeu

name: Introduction to Financial Management

description: This course is concerned with investment in stocks, bonds and other financial assets. Topics include, but are not limited to, interest rates, risk-return relationships, investment valuation, and market information and efficiency. Co-requisite: FIN 501 or FIN 503.

name: Trading and Financial Markets
description: This course will discuss the functioning of global financial markets and trading and price determination in these markets. Topics covered will include order submission and trading strategies, market structure, transaction cost and liquidity measurement, price formation, and technical 2503 analysis.

description: Intended for students pursuing the CPA designation, this course looks at derivatives, hedging and risk management. Open to students enrolled in the MAcc program only.

name: Corporate Financial Planning

name: Risk Management
description: Futures, options, and other derivative securities. Markets, valuation models, application to risk management through hedging, and the application of pricing models to the valuation of financial contracts.  It is recommended the student have credit in FIN 614. 2504

description: Financial and economic aspects of corporate mergers, restructuring, downsizing, and bankruptcy are examined. Relations between corporate structure and performance are investigated. name: Strategic Financial Management
description: Combination of comprehensive global financial management cases from the CFO perspective and presentations and discussions on current financial management topics including lectures from CFOs and others with significant expertise in global financial market and institutions.

description: Topics may vary from year to year. Students should check with the MBA Office for pre/corequisites of specific sections.

description: Consolidation des éléments travaillés au FRANC 216. Préalable: FRANC 216. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour FRANC 211.

name: Maîtrise du français
description: Axé sur les formes d'expression dans la pratique et l'académique, le cours vise à compléter le cheminement qui mène à la maîtrise de la langue française dans toutes ses nuances et complexités, tant à l'oral qu'à l'écrit. Cours axé sur la pratique s'adressant exclusivement aux étudiants dont le français n'est pas la langue première d'usage. Soumis à l'approbation du professeur. Pratique en groupe et au laboratoire de langues.

description: Pratique de la rédaction technique, journalistique et de la vulgarisation scientifique. Préalable: FRANC 226.

name: Techniques de rédaction - Français langue des affaires
description: Ce cours vise l'acquisition d'une maîtrise de la langue française spécifique au monde des affaires, afin d'optimiser la communication écrite et orale dans ce domaine, par une consolidation linguistique, méthodologique et rédactionnelle liée au contexte et aux situations du monde des affaires . Préal

name: Survol de la littérature francophone
description: Ce cours d'introduction à la littérature en français vise à faire connaître, dans une perspective sociocritique, les noms des grands écrivains et penseurs du monde francophone, leurs idées et la mise en texte de ces idées. Le corpus à l'étude consistera en des oeuvres représentatives à travers les s

name: Créativité et jeu dramatique
description: La mise en évidence des possibilités créatrices du dialogue et de la nécessité de faire découvrir, par le jeu dramatique, le fond commun et permanent de la langue parlée et de la langue écrite. Pratique de la préparation et de la mise en marche des dramatisations, afin d'explorer la création des div

name: Approfondissement langagier en contexte francophone majoritaire
description: Approfondissement du français (parlé, lu et écrit) et exploration culturelle en contexte francophone majoritaire. Par le biais d'activités d'écoute, d'interaction orale, de lecture et d'écriture, ce cours vise à permettre aux futurs enseignants d'acquérir une autonomie et une aisance langagières en

name: Théâtres francophones du Canada
description: Perspectives contemporaines sur l'évolution du théâtre francophone au Canada. Étude des oeuvres qui ont marqué son histoire et des fonctions de la langue dans les répertoires dramatiques québécois et franco-canadiens. Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour AD

name: Étude historique du français
description: Historique du français, du latin au français moderne, en tenant compte des facteurs externes (événements politiques, culturels, etc.) et des facteurs internes (évolution phonétique, syntaxique, etc.). Préalable: LINGQ 200. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des créd

name: Lecture à voix haute
description: Techniques de lecture à voix haute, exploration des ressources corporelles, vocales, musicales, visuelles et spatiales pour la lecture devant public. Survol historique de la lecture, interprétation de textes divers et procédés de mise en lecture publique. Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants

name: Pratique de la dissertation
description: La compréhension, l'analyse et la synthèse d'informations. L'étude et la manipulation du raisonnement logique. La recherche et l'exploitation des arguments. L'élaboration et la mise en forme de la dissertation comme texte clair, cohérent et convaincant. Travaux pratiques. Cours obligatoire pour l'ob

name: Introduction aux francophonies littéraires du Canada
description: Littératures du Canada francophone, des origines à nos jours, vues à travers un choix d' oeuvres marquant les diverses périodes de leur évolution. Préalable: FRANC 235. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour CA FR 350.

name: Introduction aux francophonies littéraires hors-Canada
description: Littératures du monde francophone hors-Canada, vues à travers un choix d'oeuvres et d'approches théoriques, notamment les théories postcoloniales.Préalable: FRANC 235.

name: Étude avancée du français et de l'anglais I
description: Étude comparative des systèmes français et anglais sur les plans syntaxique, morphologique, lexical et sémantique. Introduction à la traduction. Préalable(s): FRANC 221 ou l'équivalent. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour FREN 351 ou 352.

name: Étude avancée du français et de l'anglais II
description: Étude comparative des systèmes français et anglais sur les plans syntaxique, morphologique, lexical et sémantique. Introduction à la traduction, suite. Préalable(s): FRANC 331 ou l'équivalent. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour FREN 351 ou 352.

name: Littérature et société
description: Étude du traitement littéraire des grands débats de société (médias, politique, rôle social des artistes, etc.) à différentes époques dans les francophonies du monde, à travers un choix d'oeuvres. Préalable: FRANC 235.

name: Francophonies et littératures autochtones du Canada
description: Littératures écrites ou orales des Autochtones francophones ou historiquement francophones du Canada, vues à travers un choix d'oeuvres marquant leur évolution. Préalable: FRANC 235.

name: Francophonies littéraires du monde et discours identitaires
description: Étude des façons dont les discours identitaires (sexualité, nationalité, ethnie, gender, etc.) infléchissent la littérature, à travers l'étude d'un choix d'oeuvres littéraires issues de différentes francophonies du monde. Préalable: FRANC 235.

name: Littérature des femmes du monde francophone
description: Étude d'un choix d'oeuvres littéraires et critiques écrites par des femmes du monde francophone. Préalable: FRANC 235. 2509

name: Sociolinguistique du français
description: Introduction à l'étude des liens entre la langue et les facteurs sociaux (région d'origine, statut socioéconomique, genre, etc.) en contexte francophone. Les thèmes abordés incluent la variation régionale et sociale, les registres de langue, le multilinguisme et le contact linguistique. Les notions

name: Perfectionnement langagier en contexte francophone majoritaire
description: Perfectionnement du français (parlé, lu et écrit) et intégration culturelle en contexte francophone majoritaire. name: Traduction du théâtre et de la littérature au Canada
description: Initiation à la traduction littéraire et théâtrale. Étude des répertoires traduits d'une langue officielle à l'autre au Canada. Préalable(s) : *6 de niveau 300 ou 400 parmi FRANC, LINGQ. Note : Ce cours exige une bonne connaissance du français et de l'anglais.

name: Choix de sujet
description: Étude d'un sujet au choix en langue française. Préalable(s): FRANC 322 et *3 de niveau 300 en langue française.

name: Analyse syntaxique
description: Étude approfondie de la structure de la phrase française. Théorie et pratique. Préalable: LINGQ 200. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour LINGQ 470.

name: Le roman francophone du Canada depuis 1960
description: Le roman francophone de la Révolution tranquille jusqu'aujourd'hui, vu à travers un choix d'oeuvres. Préalable(s): *3 en littérature de niveau 300, préférablement FRANC 324. Note: Ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour CA FR 470.

name: Choix de sujet
description: Préalable(s): FRANC 322 et *3 en littérature ou en langue de niveau 300.

name: Francophonies du Canada: Choix de sujet en langue ou en littérature
description: Préalable(s): FRANC 228 et *3 en littérature de niveau 300, préférablement FRANC 324 ou *6 en langue française de niveau 300 ou l'approbation du Vice-doyen aux affaires académiques.

name: Francophonies littéraires du monde: Choix de sujet
description: Préalable(s): FRANC 228 et *3 en littérature de niveau 300, préférablement FRANC 330 ou l'approbation du Vice- doyen aux affaires académiques. Français: Cours de 2e cycle

name: Choix de sujet en littérature française ou francophone
description: Le contenu varie d'une année à l'autre. Les sujets sont annoncés avant la période d'inscription.
