name: Beginners' French I
description: Designed for students with little or no previous background in French. Covers material in matriculation-level French and allows students to proceed into the study of French at the University level. Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 100, or with native or near native proficiency,

name: Beginners' French II
description: Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 100, or with native or near native proficiency, or with French 30 or its equivalents in Canada and other countries.

name: Intermediate French I
description: Spoken and written French, including grammar, composition, and literature.  Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 150.

name: Intermediate French II
description: Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 150.

name: Introduction to Translation Theory and Practice: French-English-French
description: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Advanced French I
description: Designed to improve the student's command of French through intensive oral practice and advanced written exercises.  Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 251, 252 or FRANC 165, 166, 210, 211.

name: Advanced French II
description: Emphasis on the improvement of writing and speaking skills by means of numerous compositions based on texts read and discussed in class.  Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 252 or FRANC 165, 166, 210, 211. 2511

name: Introduction to French Literary Studies
description: Tools necessary to conduct literary analyses and essay writing.

name: Mystery, Myth, and Supernatural
description: Mythology, the supernatural, superstition as cultural and literary phenomena in the French-speaking world.

name: Cultural Representations of Food
description: Functions and manifestations of the food paradigm in Francophone cinematographic and narrative texts.

name: Postcolonial Issues in French and Francophone Societies
description: Francophone cultural texts from a postcolonial perspective with a focus on migration, community, exile, and identity.

name: Socio-Cultural Aspects of Contemporary Francophone Societies
description: The evolution of Francophone societies from a multidisciplinary perspective.

name: Beauty, Passions, and Obsessions
description: Addresses either a given period or a particular facet of Francophone literary texts tied to three loosely connected themes that go back to the very origins of French as a language and continue to shape cultural expression.

name: French Cultural Moments
description: Uses the study of various intellectual and historical events to provide students with a window into the French world.  Offered in La Rochelle, France only. Requires payment of additional student instructional support fees. Refer to the Fees Payment Guide in the University Regulations and Information

name: Introduction to Contemporary French Cinema and Media
description: French & Francophone cultures as expressed in media other than literature, from the invention of cinema through contemporary artistic, socio-cultural, and political forms.

name: Translation: French into English
description: Note: not to be taken by students with credit in FREN 353. This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: French Phonetics
description: Overview of the pronunciation of Standard French.

name: Topics on Language and Society in the Francophone World
description: Interactions between language and society in the Francophone world.  2512

name: Contemporary Cinema in French
description: Emphasis on the representation and evolution of society in French cinema of the last 20 years.

name: Translation: English into French
description: Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Caribbean Culture
description: Colonialism, identity, diaspora and cultural diversity in French Caribbean literature, films, and music.

name: Women Writing in French
description: Texts written in various Francophone parts of the world from different periods.

name: Topics in French Literature and Culture I
description: From medieval times through the 19th century.

name: Topics in French Literature and Culture II
description: From the 20th through the 21st century.

name: Topics in Folklore and Popular Culture
description: Study of French oral, materials, and popular culture in various parts of the Francophone world. Variable content; may be repeated for credit.

name: Special Topics
description: French Language and Literature: Graduate

name: Topics in French Literature and Culture
description: Gender and Social Justice: Graduate Faculty of Arts

name: Directed Reading in Gender and Social Justice
Social Justice Workshop
description: Advanced study of theories of social justice. Students will complete 20 hours of Community Service-Learning (CSL) and reflect on how their CSL experiences were informed by or challenged the theoretical material explored in the course.

name: Gender Research Workshop
description: Advanced study of interdisciplinary research on gender and feminist scholarship.

name: Approaches to Gender and Social Justice Studies
description: An examination of interdisciplinary approaches to feminist scholarship with an emphasis on critical analysis and knowledge production for social justice.

name: Feminist Cultural Studies
description: Examines gender as a category of analysis in contemporary cultural theory in order to examine how gendered subjects are produced and regulated through everyday cultural practices.

name: Feminist Legal Studies
description: An advanced examination of feminist legal theories and strategies with an emphasis on the contemporary North American and British context. Topics will vary. 2514

name: Feminist Theory Now
description: Examines current preoccupations within feminist theory. Topics and geographical focus will vary.

name: Feminist Historiography
description: Examines contemporary and historical approaches to writing feminist histories of a variety of regions and time periods.

name: Gender, Violence, and Social Justice in Africa
description: Critical examination of controversies around violence against African women based on insights gained from feminist debates, survivors' experiences of trauma, and studies by experts in the field.

name: Law and Feminism in Canada
description: A focus on the fundamentally contradictory role of law for women in Canada, building on the insights offered by feminist cross- disciplinary legal scholarship. (Not open to students with credit in WGS 420.)

name: Feminism and Sexual Assault
description: Interdisciplinary consideration of conceptual, political, and legal strategies that feminists have deployed to confront sexual coercion with an emphasis on the contemporary North American context. (Not open to students with credit in WGS 431.)

name: Body Politics
description: An examination of contemporary theoretical approaches to bodies and embodiment, with particular emphasis on the ways that race, class, sexuality, gender, and (dis)ability shape bodily experiences. (Not open to students with credit in WGS 440.)

name: Feminism and Religion
description: Ways in which pro-feminist women and men have constructed religious ideologies that are supportive of women's power and agency. (Not open for students with credit in WGS 455.)

name: Masculinities
description: This course surveys the status of masculinity and the emergence of contemporary masculinity studies within, alongside and, at times, against feminism and Women's Studies. (Not open to students with credit in WGS 460.)

name: Sexualities
description: Special Topics: This course offers advanced examination of selected issues in sexuality studies. (Not open to students with credit in WGS 470.)

name: Special Topics - Topics in Gender and Social Justice Studies
description: Special topics will vary.

name: Special Topics in Feminist Theory
description: This course offers advanced study of selected issues in current feminist theorizing (Not open to students with credit in WGS 499.)

name: Directed Research Project
description: Capping project. 2515 Genetics (Biological Sciences): Undergraduate Department of Biological Sciences Faculty of Science Notes 1. See the following sections for listings of other Biological Sciences courses: Bioinformatics (BIOIN); Biology (BIOL); Botany (BOT); Entomology (ENT); Microbiology (MICRB)

name: Foundations of Molecular Genetics
description: Basic concepts on the organization of genetic material and its expression will be developed from experiments on bacteria and viruses.

name: Organization of Simple Genomes
description: Two models of simple genomes will be examined. One model will focus on the function and transmission of mitochondrial DNA, the evolution of mitochondria, and the role of mitochondria in human disease and aging. The other model will focus on the application of genomics, molecular biology, and cell bi

name: Organization of Complex Genomes
description: Analysis of how eukaryotic chromosomes are organized, inherited, studied, and manipulated. Topics include: classical and current techniques, mouse genetics, epigenetics, sex chromosomes, dosage compensation, genomic imprinting, transposable elements, centromeres, telomeres, and stem cells. Prerequis

name: Gene Expression and its Regulation
description: The molecular biology of the processes by which the base sequence of genes is expressed as cellular phenotype will be examined. Emphasis will be placed upon the similarities and differences between prokaryotes and eukaryotes and upon the mechanisms that regulate the operation of particular genes.

name: Genetic Analysis
description: Analysis of gene functions in animal model systems. Mutational analysis; gene dosage; chromosome mechanics; transgenics; forward and reverse screens; dominant modifier screens; epistasis; genetic mosaics, meiotic recombination.  Credit cannot be obtained for both GENET 275 and 305.

name: Plant Genetics
description: This course examines in detail: how to induce mutations in plants by means of chemicals and transgenes; how to use mutagenized and transgenic plant populations for forward and reverse genetic approaches; how to molecularly identify genes defined by mutations in plants; how to infer gene functions an

name: Introduction to Molecular Genetics Techniques
description: A laboratory course in which students will be introduced to modern techniques in molecular genetics. These may include cytogenetics, recombinant DNA techniques, PCR, DNA sequencing, methods of detecting gene expression, and genome analysis.  Enrolment is limited, and registration is by consent of in

name: Gene Manipulation
description: Examination of fundamental techniques employed in molecular biological research relevant to both prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems. name: Beginners' German II
description: Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency, or with German 30 or its equivalents in Canada and other countries. name: Intermediate German I
description: Intended to expand comprehension and production skills in written and oral German with a focus on intercultural communicative competence.  Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency.

name: Intermediate German II
description: Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency.

name: German Cinema
description: An examination of films and cinematic movements in the German speaking world from the silent era to the new millennium within their historical, social, or political contexts. Taught in English. This course will not fulfill the Language Other than English requirement of the Faculty of Arts.

name: Shaping Modern Austria
description: The cultural and historical legacy of Austria from the Habsburgs to the present. Taught in English. This course will not fulfill the Language Other than English requirement of the Faculty of Arts.

name: Advanced German I
description: A high-intermediate to advanced-level course intended to improve overall proficiency in spoken and written German.  Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency.

name: Advanced German II
description: Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency.

name: German/English Phonetics and Phonology
description: Phonetic and phonemic analysis of English and German. Contrastive study includes application to teaching and learning. Co- requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Introduction to German Applied Linguistics
description: Broad introduction to the main fields of German applied linguistics; multilingualism, second language acquisition, sociolinguistics, and discourse analysis. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Teaching German as a Foreign Language
description: Issues relevant to teaching German as a foreign language to adult learners. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: From Masterpieces to Bestsellers
description: Interrogating the popular in texts and reception. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Introduction to the Study of Modern German Literature
description: A survey of German-language literature in context since the Enlightenment. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Introduction to Translation: German and English
description: Practice in translating texts in a variety of genres. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department. Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies. Not open to students with credit in GERM 441. 2522

name: Postwar Cultures
description: Developments in society, politics, and popular as well as high culture from 1945 to the present in German-speaking countries. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Remembering and Representing the Holocaust
description: Explores the Holocaust as a prevalent topic in international documentaries, literature, images, and films, considering theories of collective memory. Taught in English. This course will not fulfill the Language Other than English requirement of the Faculty of Arts.

name: Myths, Tales, and Legends
description: Storytelling throughout the German-speaking world since the medieval period, focusing on the development and proliferation of oral and written myths, tales, and legends. Co-requisite: GERM 303 or consent of Department.

name: Special Topics
description: Taught in English. This course will not fulfill the Language Other than English requirement of the Faculty of Arts.

name: German Dialects
description: A close look at some widely differing German dialects. Basic principles of German dialectology.

name: Second Language Acquisition: German
description: The course deals with the principles and processes in structured and unstructured language learning and with the different hypotheses and theories concerning language learning, in particular German.

name: Advanced Translation: German into English
description: Theories, methods, and strategies of translation in a variety of genres.  Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Exercises in Translation: English into German
description: Theories, methods, and strategies of translation in a variety of genres.  This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Authors in and of Their Time
description: Approaches the works of one writer, filmmaker, or artist in cultural or historical context.

name: Genre and Aesthetics
description: Examination of a genre such as epic, drama, lyric, satire, crime, melodrama, documentary, etc. and its aesthetic features.

name: Epochs and Eras
description: Discussion of a historical or cultural era on the basis of its creative products such as literature, film, or art.  2523

name: Cultural and Literary Theories
description: Explores theories either of a specific topic or by a specific scholar and their application to texts.

name: Gender and Sexuality
description: Studies texts and issues related to gender and sexuality.

name: Media and Image
description: Examines texts and products of visual culture and communication.

name: Histoire des mondes connectés depuis 1815
description: Ce cours aborde, dans une perspective globale et comparative, les principaux développements économiques, démographiques, politiques et socio-culturels qui ont marqué l'histoire du monde, de 1815 à nos jours. On y aborde en particulier comment le monde a été profondément transformé par le phénomène d

name: Histoire des États-Unis dans l'espace nord-américain
description: Ce cours est consacré à l'étude de l'histoire des États-Unis dans le contexte nord-américain (Mexique, Canada, Russie). description: Ce cours est conçu pour servir de base aux cours de niveau supérieur en histoire canadienne, l'accent étant mis sur le Canada d'expression anglaise, ses relations aux autres groupes (Français, Autochtones et les groupes de la diaspora multiculturelle), la situation des femmes au Canada, l'émigration

name: Introduction à l'étude de l'histoire du Canada de 1867 à nos jours
description: Ce cours est conçu pour servir de base aux cours de niveau supérieur en histoire canadienne et constitue la suite de HISTE 260.

description: Étude des phénomènes de migration et de mobilité des populations, à l'échelle mondiale et régionale. Ce cours aborde tant l'histoire des grands flux migratoires que les tendances contemporaines de mobilité humaine.

description: Étude de l'Afrique francophone de la décolonisation à nos jours : le poids de son passé colonial, ses défis politiques, économiques et sociaux, sa place au sein de la francophonie internationale. Préalable(s): *3 HISTE de niveau 100 ou 200.

name: Francophonies du monde
description: Étude des moments marquants et des espaces de rencontre dans l'histoire des francophonies du monde.

name: Choix de sujets en histoire du Canada
Histoire et mémoire
description: Étude du rapport histoire-mémoire et des usages sociaux du passé dans le développement des identités diverses, nationales ou communautaires.

name: Thèmes d'histoire du Canada
description: Thèmes d'histoire du Canada dans ses relations avec le monde. 2530

name: Thèmes en histoire sociale canadienne
description: Préalable(s): *3 en histoire du Canada ou l'approbation du Vice-doyen aux affaires académiques.

name: Histoire du genre et du féminisme
description: Ce cours propose d'étudier les théories du genre et d'explorer l'histoire des mouvements féministes au Canada et sur la scène internationale. Les documents historiques serviront à interroger la place des femmes à travers différentes périodes de l'histoire. Seront également étudiées les stratégies dé

name: Thèmes d'histoire de la francophonie dans l'Ouest canadien
description: Utilisation des techniques avancées de la recherche documentaire pour étudier l'histoire des francophones dans l'Ouest canadien et plus particulièrement en Alberta. Insistance sur les sources premières des archives et des musées. Préalable: HISTE 260 ou 261. History: Undergraduate Department of Hist

name: The Pre-Modern World
description: World history from the end of the 6th century to the 15th century.

name: The Early Modern World
description: World history from the 15th century through the 18th century.

name: The Modern World
description: The world since the beginning of the 19th century. Not open to students with credit in HIST 120.

name: The History of the World in the Last 10 Years
description: Global historical developments over the last 10 years with emphasis on the interaction of states and peoples. 2531

name: Technology and History
description: The role of technology in historical developments around the world.

name: The Emergence of the Atlantic World
description: The history and legacies of the transatlantic slave trade that linked Europe, Africa, and the Americas, emphasizing economic, political, social and cultural ramifications.

name: Plague: Disease and Epidemics in History
description: The causes, impacts, and experiences of disease in human history.

name: History of Sexuality in the Americas
description: History of sexual behaviour, identities, and regulations in the Western Hemisphere from the colonial era through the twentieth century.

name: Drugs in Modern Global History
description: The social, cultural, and political histories of criminalized drugs like opium, marijuana, amphetamines, and cocaine.

name: War, Revolution, and Society
description: The causes, course, and consequences of major conflicts around the globe, including their wider social effects.

name: Democracy, War and Consumer Capitalism: The Making of Modern Europe
description: European history from the Enlightenment to the present.

name: Global History of Alchemy: Quests for Gold, Power, and Immortality
description: Theories and practices of ancient and medieval alchemy in its historical and cultural contexts.

name: Video Games, History, and Storytelling
description: The use of world history in video games.

name: Capitalism
description: The development of global capitalism through an exploration of historical changes in trade, markets, ideas, work, consumption, and government policies.

name: Women and Gender in Modern Europe
description: Introduction to the history of women and gender in Europe from the eighteenth century to the present.

name: Pre-Modern Europe I
description: A survey of Europe from the fourth to the thirteenth century.

name: Pre-Modern Europe II
description: A survey of western and central European history from approximately 1200 to 1800.

name: The Early History of the British Peoples
description: Survey of the development of and relations among the societies and cultures of the British Isles from early times to 1688.

name: Britain and Its Peoples in the Modern Era
description: Survey of the major themes and issues in the formation of modern Britain from 1688 to the present.

name: Scotland from Early Times to the Present Day
description: Survey of the history of Scotland from the Reformation to the present.

name: Ireland from Early Times to the Present Day
description: Survey of the history of Ireland from St Patrick to the present.

name: The Pacific World Since 1500
description: Exploration, migration, trade, and geopolitics in the Pacific region, connecting Australia and the Pacific Islands with Asia and coastal North and South America.

name: Colonial Latin America
description: Survey of Latin American history to 1810.

name: Modern Latin America
description: Survey of Latin American history since 1810.

name: The Golden Age of Islam: History of the Muslim World to the 16th Century
description: Historical overview of the rise of Islam in seventh century Arabia and the political, economic and cultural impact of subsequent expansion into Asia, Africa and Europe.

name: Africa from Medieval to Modern Times
description: African history to the 19th century.

name: Africa in the 20th and 21st Centuries: From Colonial Rule to Modern Nations
description: African history since the 19th century.

name: American History to 1865
description: Survey of United States history from colonial times to the Civil War.

name: From the End of Slavery to the Present: American History Since 1865
description: Survey of United States history from the Civil War to the present.

name: Slavery in the Americas
description: Comparative examination of slavery in the Western Hemisphere from its creation to abolition. 2533

name: Religion in History
description: A study of a religious tradition(s), its teachings and rituals, along with its function in a specific historical setting, including its role in conditioning and reflecting a particular society and culture. Registration priority will be given to students in Honors, Majors or Minors in History.

name: East Asia to 1500
description: Survey of history of East Asia (China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam) to 1500.

name: East Asia from 1500
description: Survey of the history of East Asia (China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam) from 1500 to the present.

name: China and the West
description: A survey of Chinese-Western cultural interactions from the time of Marco Polo to the present.

name: The Chinese in Canada and Canadians in China
description: The history of the Chinese in Canada since the 1850s, and Canada's cultural and social relations with China, Hong Kong and Taiwan, through historical and literary sources, media and film.

name: Introduction to Classical India
description: The world of Classical India, from the emergence of the Mauryan Empire in the fourth century BCE to the close of the Gupta Empire in the fifth century CE.

name: Introduction to Historiography
description: Introduction to the basic concepts of historical inquiry and techniques of research and writing in History. Required for History majors.

name: World War One
description: The European conflict and its global dimensions, with an emphasis on political, social, and cultural histories.

name: History of Science, Technology and Medicine: Key Moments
description: Pivotal events in the history of science, technology, and medicine using a wide variety of historical sources, such as photographs, artifacts, films, letters, and diaries.

name: An Introduction to the History of Sciences, Technology, and Medicine
description: Broad survey of topics in the history of science, technology, and medicine.

name: 20th-Century Warfare
description: In-depth look at some of the conflicts of the 20th century, the course examines wars and revolutions including the two world wars, the Korean and Vietnam wars, African guerrilla wars, and the Gulf War. Analyze the causes and consequences of war and the evolution of weaponry. To be offered in alterna

name: World War Two
description: The global conflict, with emphasis on political, social, scientific, and cultural aspects.

name: The History of Christianity
description: Lecture and discussion course about the development of Christian traditions in the world.

name: Europe in the Age of Total War, 1890-1945
description: European experience with total war, economic crisis, cultural change and totalitarian regimes in the new era of mass politics and Great Power conflict.

name: Germany in the 20th Century and Beyond
description: The economic, social, cultural, and political histories of the various Germanies in their European and global contexts, from 1900 to the present.

name: Saints and Medieval Christianity
description: Beginning with the early Christian background, this course treats the various forms of sanctity (martyrs, monks and clerics) that appeared in medieval Europe, along with the social functions that the cult of the saints fulfilled in the world of Late Antiquity and the Middle Ages.

name: France in the 20th Century and Beyond
description: The evolution of French politics, society and culture in European and global contexts, from 1900 to the present.

name: Sexuality and Gender in Modern Europe
description: Introduction to the social organization, cultural construction, and political dimensions of sexuality and gender in Europe from the eighteenth to the twenty-first centuries.

name: The History of Paris
description: Introduction to the history of Paris from late antiquity to the present day, drawing on film, music, art, philosophy, geography, architecture, and literature.

name: A History of the Habsburg Monarchy, 1526-1918
description: The multinational empire of the Habsburgs from the unification of Austria, Bohemia and Hungary to the destruction of the empire in World War I.

name: Foundations of East European History
description: The ethnic, religious, social, and political factors which shaped the development of the peoples of Eastern Europe from the Middle Ages through the Age of Enlightenment. Intended as background to the later histories of the Balkans, 'Central Europe', and Ukraine.

name: Medieval and Early Imperial Russia
description: Russia from Kievan Rus' through Catherine the Great's reign, 900s to 1800. Note: Not open to students with credit in HIST 318. 2535

name: Modern Ukraine
description: Ukrainian history from the late eighteenth century to the present. Note: Not open to students with credit in HIST 315, 316, or 317.

name: Russia from Reform to Revolution, 1800-1917
description: Reaction, reform, and revolution, from Paul I to Nicholas II. Note: Not open to students with credit in HIST 318.

name: Russia in the 20th Century
description: An historical survey of domestic and foreign policy, from Nicholas II to Yeltsin.

name: The Middle East in the Making: 1300-1920
description: The rise and demise of the Ottoman Empire. An overview of the religious, cultural and political making of current-day North Africa, Near and Middle East, and Eastern Mediterranean. HIST 111 and 112 are recommended but not required.

name: Topics in History at the Movies
description: Provides students with the historical tools to analyze history as it is presented in movies. Topics will vary according to the instructor(s).

name: The Modern British Empire and the Commonwealth Experience
Political and Social Revolution in Latin America
description: Twentieth-century Chile, Guatemala, and Mexico. No prerequisite but HIST 242 is recommended.

name: History of Women in the United States
description: A multicultural and multiracial history of women from the colonial period to the present.

name: African American History from Slavery to Black Power
description: The African American experience in the United States from the colonial era to the present.

name: History of American Medicine
description: The social history of American health care and health practitioners. How sickness, health, and healing have changed over the course of American history.

name: Canadian Environmental History
description: Brings the natural environment onto a shared stage with social, economic, political and cultural history in Canada from the last ice age to the present.

name: History of Alberta
description: Economic, social, and political realities that underlie the many images of Alberta.

name: The Canadian West to 1885
description: Native diplomacy and warfare, the development of fur trade societies, and European colonization to the suppression of the North- West Rebellion.

name: History of the Native Peoples of Canada to 1867
description: Native cultures and societies, Native-newcomer relations, colonial Indian policy, and Native resistance to colonialism.

name: History of the Native Peoples of Canada Since 1867
description: Federal Indian policy, treaties, reserve life, Native political resurgence, and legal and constitutional developments.

name: Making War in Canada
description: History of war and conflict within Canada.

name: Peasants, Slaves and Workers
description: The history of work regimes and regulation from feudalism to the present.

name: Canada 1900 to 1945
description: Booms and depressions, world wars, social strife, and political experiments mark one of the most turbulent and critical eras in the nation's history.

name: Canada Since 1945
description: Economic, political, social and cultural developments in the postwar era.

name: Topics in the History of the United States
Religion in Modern Europe
description: Religious revival and secularization in Europe from the French Revolution to the present. Topics include: relations between church and state; intellectual challenges and responses; and religion transformed by modernity.

name: Search for a Destiny: Japan's Modern Era, 1868-Present
description: Social, political, economic and technological development; motivations, policies, obstacles and achievements are emphasized.

name: The Civilization and Culture of Early China
description: This course focuses on the formative periods of Chinese civilization from prehistory to circa 600.

name: Modern China
description: The history of China from the late nineteenth century to the present.  2537

name: History of Science in Ancient and Classical India
description: Indigenous scientific activities and achievements of ancient and classical India, from 500 BCE to 1400 CE.

name: History of Indian Yoga and Meditation
description: The history and philosophy of early Yoga, exploring its focus on meditation, its literature, its connections with Buddhism, and its historical evolution. HIST 110, 289, or CLASS 110 are recommended but not required.

name: Imperial China from circa 600 to 1911
description: The institutional and social history of imperial China from the Tang to the Manchu Ch'ing dynasties.

name: History of Technology
description: History of technology from the beginning of the Industrial era to the modern day. HIST 115, HIST 294 or STS 200 are highly recommended but not required.

name: Ancient India- Diversity in History
description: Taking the case of ancient India, this course highlights how diversity can be explained in a multicultural society and how it defines the character of civilization on the sub-continent.

name: History of Astronomy and Cosmology from Stonehenge to the Space Age
description: An examination of the major themes in the history of astronomy and cosmology from the ancient world to the present day.

name: History of Science I
description: Introduction to the intellectual, institutional, and ideological development of science, from Aristotle to the 'Scientific Revolution'.

name: History of Science II
description: Introduction to the intellectual, institutional, and ideological development of science, from Newtonianism to the present day.

name: Fashion and Material Culture c. 1600-1900
description: The evolution and practice of fashion as a social, economic, political and cultural phenomenon from a cross-cultural perspective.

name: Topics in the History of Modern Germany
Topics in the History of Early Modern Europe
description: Thematic studies in European cultural, religious, and social history emphasizing popular culture and religion.

name: Topics in the History of Europe
Topics in the History of Christianity
Topics in Latin American History Since 1850
Themes and Issues in African History
History of Edmonton
description: Themes in the history of Edmonton from the 19th century fur trade to the present day.

name: Topics in the History of the Canadian North
Topics in the History of Technology
Topics in the History of Medicine
Topics in British Empire and Commonwealth History
War and Society in the Modern World
Topics in the History of Science
History of Women and Health
description: This seminar examines the multi-cultural history of women as health practitioners, patients, and health activists in North America.

name: Methodology and Historiography for Honors Students
Special Subject, Fourth Year Honors History
description: Preparation of the Honors essay, required in the fourth year of the Honors program. History: Graduate Note: Previous study in the area is prerequisite for each course.

The Application of the Social Sciences to History
Topics in the Nature of Historical Controversy
Comprehensive Topic Course
description: Historiographical course focused on one of the department's designated graduate fields of study. May be repeated for credit when course content differs.

name: Directed Study
description: This is a credit/fail course. Not open to students in the non-thesis program.

name: Topics in the History of Later Medieval and Early Modern Europe
description: A reading knowledge of at least one of the following languages is required: Latin, German, French, Dutch or Italian.

description: Research-intensive course in which students prepare and defend an article-length primary-source-based research paper. May be repeated for credit in different years as course content necessarily differs.

name: Comprehensives and Candidacy Colloquium
description: Preparation for the comprehensive and candidacy exams.

name: Conference Course
description: Not open to graduate students in the Department of History.

name: Advanced Topics in Historical Study
description: Not open to graduate or honors students in the Department of History. 2542

name: Directed Research Project
description: ★ 3-9 (variable) (either term, unassigned) History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture, HADVC: Undergraduate Department of Art and Design Faculty of Arts Note: Because presence at lectures and seminars, participation in classroom discussion, and the completion of assignments are important components

name: Introduction to the History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture
description: Introduction to the history of art, design and visual culture.

name: Introduction to the History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture I
Introduction to the History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture II
History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in the Renaissance
description: History of art, design and visual culture in the 15th and 16th centuries, with a focus on the Italian Renaissance, covering global exchange, anatomical illustration, and the invention of the artist. Not open to students with credit in ART H 252.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in the 17th Century
description: History of art, design and visual culture in the 17th century. Highlights the visual culture of Italy, Spain, France, the Netherlands and Flanders, covering representations of the body, politics, and identity in the work of artists such as Caravaggio, Rembrandt, Velázquez, and Rubens. Not open to st

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture, 1700-1848
description: History of art, design and visual culture in Europe during the 18th and early 19th centuries, focusing on the Enlightenment and response. Includes discussion of work from the Rococo, Neoclassical, and Romantic periods.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in the Early 20th Century
description: History of art, design and visual culture from the beginnings of expressionism in the early twentieth century to the onset of the Second World War. Focuses on modernist and avant-garde modes of art and design in their historical context.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in the United States
description: History of art, design and visual culture in the United States from the Colonial period to World War II. Includes work created by and depicting women, Indigenous peoples, and ethnic minorities and addresses such issues as politics, religion, nature, and industry.

name: History of Modern Design
description: History of design from the 18th-century Industrial Revolution to the present. This course includes examples of communication, industrial, fashion and architectural design and urban planning, considering historical socio-political and environmental contexts. 2543

name: History of Photography
description: History of photography, starting with its invention in the 19th century.

name: Special Topics in the History of Art, Design and Visual Culture
description: Variable content course which may be repeated if topic(s) vary.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in Latin America
description: Introduction to the historical study of art, design and visual culture in Latin America, including Mesoamerican, pre-Hispanic Andean, Colonial, and Post-Independence cultures.

name: Perspectives on the Arts of China
description: Introduction to art history as a field of cultural production, studying the arts, design, and visual cultures in China from the neo- lithic era through today.

name: China Art Now
description: Chinese cultural identity as expressed in local and global economies of art, design, and visual culture from the Maoist era to the present.

name: China's Design Revolution
description: This course raises the question: when will China stop manufacturing and start designing? Students will study the historical foundations, theory and practice of sustainable design in China since the end of the Maoist era.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in Japan
description: Introduction to the historical study of art, design, and visual culture in Japan. Includes painting, sculpture, architecture, gardens, decorative arts, prints, and anime and addresses such concerns as identity, nation, tradition, and modernity.

name: Gender, Sexuality and Visual Culture
description: Introduction to key themes in feminist art, design and visual culture. Examines the production and circulation of visual markers of difference (such as race, gender, sexuality, ability, size, etc.) through the media, popular culture, and contemporary art.

name: History of Art, Design and Visual Culture in the Mid-20th Century
description: History of art, design and visual culture in the context of the Cold War and consumer society. Focuses on the persistence of modernism in the postwar era and the diverse challenges to it from 1940 to 1975. Not open to students with credit in ART H 256 (*3) offered prior to 2012-13.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture, 1848-1900
description: This course examines art, design and visual culture in Europe from the socially and politically turbulent period of 1848 to the end of the long 19th century. Includes discussion of Realism, Impressionism, Symbolism, and Art Nouveau.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in the Contemporary Era
description: Introduction to key issues in art, design, and visual culture from 1970 to the present, including art and identity, performance and politics, social sculpture, institutional critique, new media, bio art, relational aesthetics, social practice, and the pedagogical turn.

name: History of Art, Design, and Visual Culture in Canada
description: History of art, design and visual culture in Canada, from the colonial period to the present. Includes work created by and depicting women and Indigenous peoples, addressing such issues as nature, religion, gender, and national identity. 2544

name: Geographies of Art, Design, and Visual Culture
description: The history of art, design and visual culture of a particular geographic location (e.g. nation, city, region, continent).  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200- level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

name: Modernism and Modernity
description: Aspects of the modern as it applies to the artistic practices of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200-level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

name: Design Theory and History
description: Historical and/or contemporary issues in design practice and theory.  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200- level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

name: Issues in the History of Art, Design and Visual Culture
description: Students are expected to have successfully completed two 200-level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.  Variable content course which may be repeated if topic(s) vary.

name: Chinese Urban Art and Design Now
description: The historical and contemporary Chinese city-as representation, model, catalyst, and socio-political construct.  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200- level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

name: Art and Institutions
description: The relationship between objects and their modes of interpretation, circulation and display (e.g. museums, academies, exhibitions, etc.)  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200-level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

name: Introduction to Performance Art
description: Introduction to performance art of the 20th and 21st centuries.  Note: Students are required to have successfully completed two 200- level HADVC courses with a minimum grade of B-.

description: Note: Students are required to have successfully completed one 300-level HADVC course with a minimum grade of B.

Human Ecology: Undergraduate Department of Human Ecology Faculty of Agricultural, Life and Environmental Sciences Note: Enrolment in some HECOL cours

name: Introduction to Principles and Practice in Human Ecology
description: An introductory course that provides a foundation in the body of knowledge that constitutes the field of human ecology. The history, philosophy, theoretical approaches and scope of the field are explored and skills that foster effective professional practice are discussed.

name: Introduction to Textiles
description: An introductory exploration of the origin, creation and use of textiles in our world. Chemical and physical properties of fibres and fabrics are introduced and an understanding of how these properties influence various clothing and textile end uses is developed.

name: Introduction to Material Culture
description: The study of a range of objects and environments (from clothing to buildings) within selected time periods, and the study of particular perspectives through which such items can be interpreted. Students explore and learn about the connections between personal, social, temporal, and cultural contexts

name: Intimate Relationships
description: A consideration of the sociological, psychological, and personal factors affecting the development, maintenance and dissolution of intimate relationships today.

name: Human Sexuality
description: An inquiry into the nature of sexual behavior, its personal and cultural sources, and the personal, familial and societal implications.

name: Seniors and Their Environments
description: An introduction to the environments in which older people live. The course uses an ecological framework to study the symbolic, physical, interpersonal, community, and political environments of older adults in Canada and other world regions.

name: Fashion Industries
description: An introduction to the soft goods industry including an overview of the apparel sector, apparel production, channels of distribution, fashion oriented products, global competitive influences, and career opportunities. 2547

name: Design Studies and Practice
description: Explores the production of artifacts as material culture from a human ecology perspective. Artifact production focuses on the design process including visualization, communication, inspiration/influence and interface. Students are introduced to the production of two-and three- dimensional design thr

name: Apparel Design and Construction Fundamentals
description: In this studio course students develop fundamental awareness and skills for the textiles and clothing field including machine operation, design and construction terminology, pattern manipulation and fit, and quality construction techniques. Students construct samples and garments with a focus on pro

name: Survey of Historic Dress in the Western World
description: Introduction to the historical development of dress in the Western World with contemporary applications in design, merchandising, arts performance, education and museums. Resources include the Clothing and Textiles Collection.

name: Applications of Textile Science
description: Advancement of textile concepts introduced in HECOL 170 with an emphasis on textile finishing, colouration, care and maintenance. Techniques for fibre identification and yarn and fabric structural analysis are covered.

name: Policy Development and Evaluation
description: Processes of policy development, implementation and analysis; Canadian policy environments, institutional frameworks and instruments; application to professional practice and to current social and economic issues.

name: Program Planning and Evaluation
description: Theories and processes of program planning, implementation, and evaluation from a human ecological perspective.

name: Parent-Child Relationships
description: An exploration of parent-child relationships, with a concentration from infancy through adolescence. An examination of theoretical and research perspectives of parent-child relationships and the practical application of those perspectives.

name: Family Dynamics
description: An introduction to family dynamics from the perspective of family theory. Changes in family dynamics across the life course will be examined using theoretical concepts, research, and practical application of concepts.

name: Interviewing and Counseling
description: An introduction to interviewing and counseling strategies for working with individuals using a strengths-based, human ecological approach.

name: Introduction to Family Finance
description: An introduction to the principles of money management for individuals, households, and families. Students learn basic financial literacy skills and tools required to make key financial decisions by identifying financial goals, assessing current resources, developing and implementing a financial plan

name: Family Economic Issues
description: An examination of current issues affecting the economic well-being of Canadian families and of government policies which address those issues. Issues explored include work and family; the economics of childbearing, education, delayed life transitions, and aging; intrafamily allocation of resources;

name: Cross-Cultural Textiles
description: An introduction to the historical and technological development of cross-cultural textiles and survey of common textile-making techniques, designs, and embellishments. Ethnographic textiles are analyzed in the context of socio-cultural, political, economic, environmental, and aesthetical meanings. T

name: Apparel Design and Product Development I
description: Principles of design and merchandising applied to apparel design and portfolio development. A creative problem-solving approach to production of a line of clothing.  Normally offered in alternate years.

name: Dress and Culture
description: The complex phenomenon of bodily adornment is explored in relationship to values, attitudes, activities, beliefs, and forms of knowledge. Clothing is considered in terms of how it is expressive of various aspects of culture. Students develop analytical skills to help them understand the role played

name: Quality Assurance for Textiles and Apparel
description: Exploration of quality assurance of textiles and apparel through materials testing. Performance of textiles relative to product standards and specifications.

name: Intentional Professional Practice
description: Preparation for human ecology practicum placement. Effective workplace relationships and issues involved in professional practice are explored within the context of being a practicum student. Reflective practice and career development are key concepts of the course.

name: Practicum in Human Ecology
description: Supervised field experience. Students are placed in professional settings appropriate to their academic background and career goals. Participation in a weekly online seminar is required.  Requires payment of additional student instructional support fees. Refer to the Fees Payment Guide in the Univer

name: Family Challenges
description: An in-depth exploration of several family challenges (e.g. addiction, homelessness, and childhood and adult abuse). An introduction to specific prevention and intervention approaches related to family challenges.

name: Working With Families
description: Assessment and counseling strategies for working with families using a strengths-based, family-centred approach.  2549

name: Families and Aging
description: Explores issues in mid and later life families including intergenerational and interpersonal relationships and family care.

name: Family Policy Issues
description: Analysis of current policy issues faced by Canadian families and the examination of policies and programs affecting family well- being and relationships.

name: Textiles and Apparel in the Global Economy
description: Production and distribution of textiles and apparel in a global context; issues and policy related to international trade agreements; impact of national and international consumer, labor and environmental standards.

name: Family Law
description: Family law regulates intimate and domestic relationships. Examines from a user's perspective, how statutory and common law affects family relationship issues such as marriageand divorce, child custody and child welfare, adoption, and new reproductive technologies.  Offered in alternate years.

name: Apparel Design and Product Development II
description: Advanced problems in apparel design and product development. Draping and fabric manipulation techniques are explored. The focus is traditional specialty fabric techniques and experimental production methods.  Normally offered in alternate years.

name: Nineteenth, Twentieth, and Twenty-First Century Dress in the Western World
description: Advanced study of Western dress from the 19th century to the present. Lectures and labs introduce students to artifactual research and the handling, storage, examination, documentation and exhibition of artifacts. Resources include fashion plates, photographic archives and artifacts from the Univers

name: Material Culture in Home and Community
description: Framed within the context of theories in human ecology, this course investigates material culture ranging from individual artifacts to community, including local and global environments. The roles and effects of material culture on individual, familial and community living are explored through liter

name: Fashion and Material Culture: Politics, Economies, Societies, c. 1600-1900
description: Examines the evolution and practice of fashion as a social, economic, political and cultural phenomenon from a cross-cultural perspective.  Normally offered in alternate years.

name: Research Methods for Material Culture and Design Studies
description: Explores how to do research into the relationships between people and objects (from clothing to buildings). Students will learn how to conduct primary research through a range of methods, approaches, and theories associated with material culture and design studies. Credit will only be given for one

name: Material Culture in Practice
description: Explores how human ecological and material culture approaches contribute to the analysis of a range of contemporary issues in design practice.

name: Topics in Advanced Textile and Apparel Science
description: Advanced topics in functional textiles and protective clothing are addressed. Resistance of textiles to mechanical, thermal, chemical, biological, and electrical hazards as well as questions of durability and comfort are discussed with an emphasis on current research. The course describes phenomena

name: Clothing and Materials for Sport and Safety
description: A human ecological exploration of the selection, use and performance of clothing and materials used for workplace safety protection and sporting/recreational activities.  Normally offered in alternate years.

name: Introduction to Nanotechnologies and Smart Textiles
description: Students explore opportunities offered by nanotechnologies and smart materials/structures as they relate to textile products. Mechanisms involved, materials used, fabrication processes, properties/performance obtained, and some examples of commercial applications are presented. Challenges encountere

description: Independent project or study of a topic in human ecology planned by the student with an instructor. Open to human ecology students only.

description: Normally offered in Spring or Summer. Can be taken for credit more than once if the topics are different.

name: Independent Project in Human Ecology
description: Independent study of a topic in human ecology planned by the student in consultation with the Instructor. Independent studies may be taken more than once for credit. 2551

description: Topics of current interest. May be taken for credit more than once.

name: Review of Literature in Clothing, Textiles and Material Culture Studies
description: Review of scholarly literature related to clothing and textiles sciences and/or material culture research. A literature review is a required part of this course. Not to be taken if credit received for HECOL 680.

name: Fundamentals of Human Comfort in Protective Clothing
description: Examination of heat and mass transfer between clothing and the human body in relation to comfort and protection.

Theory rel

name: Research Methods in Textile and Apparel Science
description: An examination of qualitative and quantitative research methods relevant to clothing and textiles scholarship.

name: Ways of Knowing in Human Ecology
description: Enquiry into the nature, scope and object of human ecology knowledge; the distinct contributions of various modes of inquiry; and the relationship between ways of knowing and selected issues related to the acquisition of knowledge, such as ethics and research methods.

name: Fundamentals of Aging
description: A critical analysis of the issues and environments that influence the lives of older Canadians. Focus is on theories and knowledge about age-related normative and non-normative changes and their interaction with the physical, social, community and policy environments of older adults.

name: Review of Issues and Trends in Family Ecology and Practice
description: Content and philosophy of the study of the family from a human ecological perspective.

name: Theory in Family Ecology
description: Consideration of family theory as it relates to research and practice. Pre or corequisite: HECOL 610 or consent of Instructor.

name: Family Challenges
description: An in-depth exploration of several family challenges (e.g., addiction and homelessness). An introduction to specific intervention approaches related to family challenges.

name: Graduate Practicum in Human Ecology
description: ★ 3-6 (variable) (variable, variable) Selected practicum placements to integrate theory and practice in a variety of agencies. name: Families and Aging
description: Current issues in mid- and later-life families including relationships between aging parents and adult children, grandparent relationships, family caregiving. 2553

description: In-depth examination of intimate relationships, including theoretical perspectives, research methods, relationship forms and processes, and how context affects relationships. description: An examination of the complex relationship between two important social institutions: work and family. description: An examination of the elements of policies and policy issues that affect contemporary Canadian families. name: Research Project
description: Directed research done under the supervision of an academic member in the Faculty of Kinesiology, Sport, and Recreation or approved research affiliate. Normally for students in their fourth year of study.  Students must arrange a project with an academic staff member or approved research affiliate.

name: Directed Studies

name: Introduction to Sociology of Sport and Leisure in Canadian Society
description: This course brings a sociological imagination to the study of sport and leisure with particular reference to Canadian society. Note: Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 104 or PERLS 104.

name: Introduction to the Management of Sport, Physical Activity and Recreation Programs
description: Provides students with an introduction to the management concepts required to successfully administer a sport, recreation or physical activity. Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 105 or PERLS 105.

name: Canadian History of Leisure, Sport, and Health
description: An introductory examination of Canadian leisure, sport, physical cultures, recreation, tourism, and health, in a global world, since the 19th century. name: Adapted Physical Activity and Leisure for Diverse Populations
description: An introduction to research, theory and practice pertaining to participation in physical activity and leisure by persons with impairments. description: An examination of the specific role played by volunteer management in the delivery of recreation, sport and physical activity programs, including the structure and processes of the voluntary organizations that make up the recreation delivery system.  Note: Credit will be granted for only one of RLS

name: Advanced Analysis of Sport and Leisure Organizations
description: Theoretical consideration for the organization and administration of exercise, sport, recreation, and leisure programs.  Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 350 or PERLS 350.

name: Leisure Facilities: Planning and Management

name: Assessment and Evaluation in Physical Activity for Children and Youth

name: The Business of Hockey
description: This course explores strategic, economic, and cultural issues related to the business of hockey - with a specific focus on the National Hockey League. Students will develop a critical understanding of the hockey industry and its stakeholders.  Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 411 or PERLS

name: Play: The Foundation of Recreation, Sport and Physical Activity
description: This course will provide students with an in-depth understanding of the concept of play. It will offer learning experiences that will enable students to create play in various recreation, sport, tourism and physical activity contexts.  Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 420 or PERLS 420.

name: Play Leadership
description: The content will include the defining criteria and values of Play Leadership. The roles and responsibilities of Play Leaders in fostering learning and development through play will be examined. Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 421 or PERLS 421.

name: Play Around the World Program Preparation
description: The "Play Around the World" project provides a 3-month international or Canadian based, cross-cultural field placement working with children and youth of all abilities in the area of play, recreation and sport. name: Play Around the World - Field Placement
description: The "Play Around the World" project provides a 3-month international or Canadian based, cross-cultural field placement working with children and youth of all abilities in the area of play, recreation and sport. Students apply and are selected in Fall Term, and then have a significant time commitment

name: Sport, Leisure and Social Theory
description: An advanced seminar course that engages select social and cultural theories to examine critically contemporary sport and leisure practices.  Students may not receive credit for both KRLS 351 and KRLS 451. Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 451 or PERLS 451.

name: Senior Research Experience
description: This is an independent study course that allows students to explore a research topic under the supervision of a Doctoral student. Students will use the resources of libraries, laboratories, and current research projects with the goal of gaining an understanding of the process of conducting research.

name: Selected Topics in Physical Education, Recreation and Leisure Studies
description: A course offered on a topic of current interest in physical education and sport. Topics may vary from year to year.  Credit will be granted for only one of KRLS 497 or PERLS 497. Kinesiology, Recreation, Leisure, and Sport: Graduate

name: Torts

name: Constitutional Law

name: Trusts

Will provide a brief historical review and discuss new developments

name: Advanced Criminal Law
description: The course comprises an examination of substantive criminal law particularly: offences against the person and rights of property; the jury system; juvenile justice and quasi-criminal proceedings; and, the extraordinary remedies.

name: International Environmental Law

description: This is an academic methods and theory seminar for graduate students. N

description: Practical application of skills to on-going language documentation and revitalization projects.

name: Field Methods
description: Practical experience in linguistic data collection and analysis of the sound and form systems of an unfamiliar language.  Not offered every year.

description: A study of recent developments in particular areas of linguistic research.  Formerly LING 443. Linguistics: Graduate

name: Pharmaceutical Formulation and Development

name: Advanced Pharmacokinetics

name: The Metabolism and Excretion of Drugs

name: Introduction à la philosophie occidentale

Note: ce cours n'est pas accessible aux étudiants ayant ou postulant des crédits pour PHILE 1

name: La bioéthique
description: Regard philosophique sur les problèmes majeurs de la bioéthique. Exemples: les droits et les devoirs du personnel hospitalier et du patient, l'euthanasie active et passive, le droit à la vie et l'avortement, la recherche et l'expérimentation en médecine humaine et animale, la manipulation génétique.

name: Introduction to Philosophy: Values and Society
description: An introduction to the classical problems of philosophy through study and critical discussion of selected philosophical classics and contemporary works. Emphasis will be placed on questions of moral and other values and on the nature of society and justice. 2776

name: Introduction to Philosophy: Knowledge and Reality
description: An introduction to the classical problems of philosophy through study and critical discussion of selected philosophical classics and contemporary works. Emphasis will be placed on questions of the nature and extent of human knowledge and classic problems about the nature of reality and our place in

name: Symbolic Logic I
description: A study of sentential logic, including translation, semantics, decision procedures and natural deduction followed by an introduction to predicate logic, concentrating on translation. Note: Not open to students with credit in PHIL 220.

name: Practical Logic
description: Elementary methods and principles for analyzing reasoning as it occurs in everyday contexts. Topics may include informal fallacies, introduction to scientific method, elementary statistical reasoning, elementary sentential logic, as well as the study of argument in contemporary debates about issues

name: Metaphysics
description: Basic questions concerning the nature of reality. Topics may include existence, materialism and idealism, freedom and determinism, appearance and reality, causality, identity, time and space, universals and particulars.

name: Philosophy of Mind
description: Basic questions concerning the mind and our attempts to study it scientifically.

name: The Human Person: Philosophical Issues
description: Personal identity, interpersonal relationships, sex and gender, freedom and immortality in historical and contemporary contexts.

name: Epistemology
description: A study of such central topics in the theory of knowledge as truth and rationality, skepticism and the limits of knowledge, relativism and the objectivity of knowledge, the role of perception, memory and reason as sources of knowledge.

name: Biology, Society, and Values
description: The philosophical and social impact of historical and contemporary topics in the biological sciences.

name: Symbolic Logic II
description: A brief review of sentential logic followed by an intensive study of predicate logic with identity. Topics include translation, semantics, decision procedures, natural deduction systems, mathematical induction. Other topics include: theories of definite descriptions, elementary modal logic, formal a

name: Ancient Greek Philosophy
description: A survey of the thought of the ancient Greek world from its beginnings with the Pre-Socratics up to and including Aristotle.

name: Trial and Execution of Socrates
description: A philosophical examination of Socrates' death in its cultural, historical and political setting.

name: Greek Philosophy and the Christian Tradition
description: Issues concerning human beings, knowledge, ethics and society among Greek thinkers and their impact on Christian thought. Note: Not available for credit with PHIL 139. 2777

name: Descartes to Hume
description: A survey of Philosophy in the 17th- and 18th centuries. Philosophers studied will include Descartes, Leibniz, Spinoza, Locke, Berkeley, and Hume.

name: Medieval Philosophy and the Christian Tradition
description: A survey of philosophy from the 5th to the 15th centuries; Philosophers from the Jewish, Islamic and Christian traditions.

name: Contemporary Ethical Issues
description: An examination of questions of right and wrong, good and evil, and the application of ethical theories to practical issues.

name: Philosophy of Science
description: An introduction to the central issues in contemporary philosophy of science. Topics may include theory evaluation, paradigm shifts and theory change, laws of nature, causation and explanation, the rationality of science and its social and historical setting.

name: Moral Issues in a Christian Context
description: Analysis and evaluation of selected moral and social issues.

name: Political Philosophy
description: A survey of issues in contemporary political philosophy with attention to liberalism and communitarianism, sovereignty, feminism, entitlement and distribution, and global justice.

name: Feminist Philosophy
description: An introduction to feminist issues in current philosophy. Note: Not open to students with credit in PHIL 332.

name: Philosophy of Hunting
description: The moral, conceptual, existential, environmental, socio-political, and spiritual issues raised by the practice of hunting by humans.

name: Philosophy of Art
description: An introduction to some of the traditional theories, such as the expressionist and the formalist theories, which investigate the nature and function of the arts. The nature of aesthetic experience will also be considered.

name: Existentialism
description: An introduction to the background and main themes of existentialist philosophy. Authors such as Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Heidegger, and Sartre are considered.

name: Indian Philosophy
description: This course focuses on the ancient and classical period of Indian philosophy (500 BCE-1500 CE); major metaphysical and ethical concepts within traditions such as Yoga, Advaita Vedanta, and Buddhism. Note: Not open to students with credit in PHIL 301 completed prior to 2012.

name: Philosophy of Psychology
description: Central topics at the interface of philosophy and psychology.  2778

name: Risk, Choice, and Rationality
description: A study of the formal theory of rationality including probability and induction, and elementary decision theory, with attention to the paradoxes of choice.

name: Natural Philosophy and the Christian Tradition
description: A critical study of physical reality, dealing with such concepts as nature, scientific knowledge, space, time, causality, biological life, and teleology, in traditional and contemporary contexts.

name: Aristotle
description: An in-depth study of the philosophy of Aristotle.

name: Medieval and Renaissance Philosophy
description: A historical survey of western philosophy from the end of classical antiquity until the sixteenth century.

name: Contemporary World Views and Christianity
description: Critical study of Christianity in dialogue with such worldviews as atheism, agnosticism, naturalism, materialism, existentialism, feminism, liberalism, postmodernism.

name: Kant to Nietzsche
description: A survey of the philosophy of Kant and the 19th century. Philosophers studied will include Kant, Hegel, Marx, the Utilitarians, and Nietzsche. Note: Not open to students with credit in PHIL 245.

name: Humans and Animals
description: Philosophical approaches to the question of comparative human and animal cognition, emotion, awareness, and language. The course will also address the problem of animal rights vis-à-vis individual and institutional human interests.

name: Foundations of Ethics
description: A philosophical investigation of theoretical questions about ethics, such as whether ethical values are objective or subjective, why we should be moral, whether virtues really exist, what role reason plays in ethical deliberation, and what constitutes the basis of our ethical obligations.

name: Environmental Ethics
description: Philosophical dimensions of issues raised by our relationship to the environment.

name: Philosophy of Religion
description: General topics in the Philosophy of Religion, which may include the concept of 'religion,' the existence of God, meaning and intelligibility in religious language, religion and morality, implications of the social scientific study of religion.

name: Philosophy of Computing
description: Emphasis on artificial intelligence, artificial life, and virtual reality. No previous familiarity with computing is necessary.

name: Computers and Culture
description: A philosophical examination of moral and social issues arising from the computer revolution. Possible topics include hacking, internet culture, smart environments and cyborgs.

name: Introduction to Philosophy of Mathematics
description: Overview of approaches to the foundations of mathematics and of philosophical issues concerning the nature of mathematical objects, mathematical theories and the special status of mathematics among the sciences and other areas of knowledge.

name: Topics in Social Justice
description: Variable content course which may be repeated if topic(s) vary.

name: Film and Philosophy
description: Study of philosophical issues raised by films: distinguishing film from other arts (photography, theatre, video games), whether films depict or represent reality, emotional engagement, the relation of moral and aesthetic values, and approaches to film criticism.

name: Topics in Practical Ethics
description: Variable content course which may be repeated if topic(s) vary.

name: Health Care Ethics
description: A study of ethical issues that arising in health care and in the practice of health professions.

name: Philosophy and Nursing II: Christian Perspectives
description: ★ 1.5 (fi 3) (either term, 18 hours) Examining moral and social issues surrounding the goals of nursing and of healthcare from a Christian perspective, using traditional bioethics principles and complementary approaches (e.g. ethics of care, virtue ethics, etc.). name: Metalogic

name: Modal Logic

name: Philosophy of Language

name: 17th- and 18th-Century British Philosophy
description: Topics concerning the early modern British philosophical tradition of Locke, Berkeley, and Hume.

Préalable(s) : PSYCE ou PSYCO 104 ou SCI 100 et PSYCE ou PSYCO 105. Note : Les sujets et les préalable

name: Psychologie de la santé
description: L'étude des différents modèles théoriques en psychologie de la santé, des effets du stress et des représentations de la maladie, ainsi que leur application à des comportements et des problèmes de santé. Les sujets sont abordés sous un angle biopsychosocial et incluent la promotion de la santé, les m

description: Introduction to the study of human individuality, personality, and social psychological processes. Fulfillment of the ¼ laboratory credit typically entails serving as a research participant

description: Psychological principles and processes as they relate to training, including research, design and analysis, lifespan development, memory and cognitive processing, social psychological processes, personality, psychological disorders and their treatment. Note: Open only to students enrolled in Nursing

name: Introduction to Research Methods in Psychology
description: Experimental and nonexperimental methods in psychology. Topics covered include philosophy of science, measurement, reliability and validity of methods, measures, and effects; experimental quasi-experimental, and single-subject designs; biases in experimentation; and research ethics.  [Faculty of Art

name: Lifespan Developmental Psychology
description: Introduction to biological, cognitive and social aspects of psychological development.  [Faculty of Arts]

description: Nature and treatment of psychological disorders, such as cross-disciplinary perspectives and an emphasis on improving understanding of psychopathology in everyday life.  Not open to students with credit in PSYCO 339. [Faculty of Arts]

name: Social Psychology
description: A survey of theories and research on the individual in a social context.  Note: PSYCO 241 and SOC 241 may not both be taken for credit. [Faculty of Arts] 2825

name: Cognitive Psychology
description: A survey of findings of theoretical issues in the study of cognition, such as perception, attention, knowledge representation, memory, learning, language, reasoning, and problem solving.  [Faculty of Science]

name: Brain and Behavior
description: An introduction to brain mechanisms involved in sensation, perception, movement, motivation, learning, and cognition, as studied in both humans and lower animals.  [Faculty of Science]

name: Behavior Modification

name: Honors Seminar I
description: A range of conceptual and methodological issues in psychology are considered, and students receive intensive training and practice in both written and oral communications. The seminar meets once a week for the full Fall/Winter period. Restricted to, and required of, third-year students in the Arts H

name: Special Topics in Psychological Research
description: Review and discussion of special topics or methods in one or more of the areas of contemporary psychology such as experimental, perception, physiological, learning, memory, behavior, quantitative.  Students must check with the Department for the topics for the year and any additional prerequisites.

name: History of Ideas in Psychology
description: Psychological ideas from ancient times to the beginning of cognitive science in the mid 20th century.  [Faculty of Arts]

name: History of Modern Psychology
description: An overview of the scientific discipline of psychology since the mid-20th century.  [Faculty of Science]

name: Special Topics in Psychology I
description: Review and discussion of special topics or methods in one or more of the areas of contemporary psychology such as developmental, social, personality, cognitive.  Note: Consult with the Department for the specific topic offered each year and any additional prerequisites. [Faculty of Arts] 2826

name: Honors Seminar I
description: A range of conceptual and methodological issues in psychology are considered, and students receive intensive training and practice in both written and oral communications. The seminar meets once a week for the full Fall/Winter period. Pre-requisite: A committed Thesis Supervisor and approval of the

name: Infant and Child Development
description: Biological, cognitive and social aspects of psychological development during the period from infancy to childhood.  [Faculty of Arts]
