description: Integration of developmental theories and research with an emphasis on practical approaches to support healthy development. Students volunteer with a local agency to work with infants, children, or adolescents. May be offered as a Community-Service Learning course.  [Faculty of Arts].

name: Adolescent Development
description: Biological, cognitive, and social aspects of development that occur during the period from early to late adolescence.  [Faculty of Arts]

name: Adult Development and Aging
description: Overview of physical, psychological, and sociocultural realities of aging. This course may be offered as a Community Service- Learning course.  [Faculty of Arts]

name: Personality Theory
description: An in-depth treatment of psychological theories of personality.  Note: Not open to students with credit in PSYCO 233 or PSYCO 305 Topic: Personality. [Faculty of Arts].

name: Introduction to Clinical Psychology
description: The study of the profession of clinical psychology, including topics such as case studies to examine diagnosis and assessment, judgement and decision making, and psychotherapeutic and community interventions.  Not open to students with credit in PSYCO 435. [Faculty of Arts]

name: Cultural Psychology
description: Study of cultural variations in psychological processes such as self-perception, cognition, emotion, and motivation. The course also introduces the issues of cultural evolution and multiculturalism.  [Faculty of Arts]

name: Social Influence
description: Introduction to research on social influence, including theory and empirical research on conformity, compliance, and obedience.  Note: Not open to students with credit in PSYCO 305 Topic: Social Influence. [Faculty of Arts].

name: Intercultural Communication
description: Review of theory and research on communication processes in intercultural social interactions.  Note: Not open to students with previous credit in PSYCO 405 Topic: Intercultural Communications [Faculty of Arts]. 2827

name: Interpersonal Relationships
description: A general overview of research and theory in the domain of relationships, based on classic and contemporary findings from the social psychological literature. Most of the course content will focus on adult romantic relationships.  Note: Not open to students with credit in HECOL 210 or PSYCO 305 Topi

name: Human Memory

The emphasis will be on developing coherent theor

name: Spatial Cognition

name: Foundations of Cognitive Science
description: An introduction to the theories and research practices of cognitive science by examining contributions of cognitive psychology, artificial intelligence, linguistics, and neuroscience to a variety of research areas.  [Faculty of Science]

name: Research Methods in Cognition
description: A detailed examination of some of the common methods used for investigating cognitive processes. Topics include response time methods, priming paradigms, tachistoscopic presentation techniques, reading time measurement, and the use of recognition and recall tests. The focus of the course will be on

name: Language Processing

name: Perception

Topics include perception, motor control, attention, memory

name: Principles of Learning

name: Honors Thesis I: Research Apprenticeship

name: Evolutionary Theory in Psychology
description: An introduction to evolutionary psychology; the course will examine the application of evolutionary theory to the field of psychology.

name: Individual Research

Exploration de quelques théories et concepts, tels que les formes organisationnelles et règles qui les régissent, l'identité au travail, la culture d'entreprise, l'environnement organisationnel, le système d'autorité et de pouvoir, la division du travail, etc.

name: Sociologie des rapports de sexes
description: Étude comparée des rapports entre les femmes et les hommes dans certaines sociétés, en mettant l'accent sur le Canada contemporain; étude des rôles spécifiques à chaque sexe, et des théories relatives à leurs origines; recherche sociologique récente sur l'importance de la division sexuelle de la soc

name: Sociologie des média et de l'information
description: La place des média et des nouvelles technologies de l'information dans la société contemporaine. Étude des théories qui s'y rattachent, avec l'accent sur les débats entourant la question de la postmodernité. Préalable: SOCIE 100.

name: Étude des minorités et des groupes ethniques
description: Analyse de processus sociaux qui permettent le développement et la compréhension du statut des minorités. Étude de cas des relations entre les groupes ethniques et minoritaires fondée sur les travaux réalisés à l'échelle nationale. Préalable: SOCIE 100.

name: La famille
description: Le système familial vu dans le contexte de l'histoire et de la rencontre des cultures. Étude du système familial dans les sociétés contemporaines, soulignant les aspects caractéristiques de l'institution et ses tendances actuelles.

name: Théories sociologiques contemporaines
description: Étude de théories sociologiques contemporaines : Phénoménologie, constructivisme, fonctionnalisme, systémisme, théorie du genre et féminisme, etc. Préalable(s): SOCIE/SOC 100 ou autre cours de sciences sociales de niveau 200 ou plus.

name: Sociologie du développement
description: Analyse critique des enjeux de développement dans l'économie mondiale et le système interétatique; analyse de différents aspects des sociétés en voie de développement: régimes agraires et monde rural; stratégies d'industrialisation; marché du travail (secteur formel/informel); clivages sociaux (clas

name: Introductory Sociology
description: An examination of the theory, methods, and substance of Sociology. The study of how societies are shaped including economy, culture, socialization, deviance, stratification, and groups. The process of social change through social movements, industrialization, etc. Note: Not to be taken by students w

name: Social Problems
description: Selected structural issues in various societies, including inequality, population growth, environment, and human rights. Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in SOC 102.

name: Introduction to Social Statistics
description: Statistical reasoning and techniques used by sociologists to summarize data and test hypotheses. Topics include describing distributions, cross-tabulations, scaling, probability, correlation/regression and non-parametric tests.  Note: This course is intended primarily for students concentrating in S

name: Classical Social Theory
description: The foundational contributions and relevance of the works of Marx, Weber, Durkheim and others to sociology and social theory. Course emphasizes close reading of primary texts to cultivate reading, writing and reasoning skills.

name: Sociology of Deviance and Conformity
description: Processes involved in defining behaviour patterns as deviant; factors that influence conformity and change; examination of behaviour patterns such as sexuality, alcoholism, drug use, and selected mental and physical disabilities; public reaction to such behaviour.

name: Criminology
description: Examination and attempted explanation of crime and juvenile delinquency, with an analysis of the social processes leading to criminal behaviour.

name: Social Studies of Surveillance
description: Critical analysis of the increased prominence of diverse forms of surveillance in contemporary society.

name: Social Psychology
description: An introduction to the study of individual and group behaviour observed in social processes.  Note: SOC 241 and PSYCO 241 may not both be taken for credit.

name: Biologically Coordinated Social Psychology
description: A biologically consistent introduction to the study of individual and group behaviour observed in social processes.

name: Population and Society
description: Population trends, issues and concerns in Canada and international contexts; social and cultural factors underlying fertility, mortality, and migration; urbanization; population change; population theory; and demographic analysis.

name: Inequality and Social Stratification
description: Introduction to the study of structured social inequalities and poverty; major theoretical approaches; findings from key empirical studies, with emphasis on Canada.

name: Introductory Sociology of Globalization
description: Introduces various aspects of globalization and its impact on our lives at local, national, and international levels.  2888

name: Introduction to the Family
description: An introduction to the study of family relationships and their variant forms with focus on mate selection, couple, kin, age, and gender dynamics, family dissolution or reconstitution and change. A comparative approach with emphasis on families in Canada.

name: Introduction to Environmental Sociology
description: Sociological examination of the relationship between human societies and the natural environment.

name: Sociology of Gender
description: Comparative study of sex roles in selected societies with an emphasis on contemporary Canada; sex-specific role behaviours and theories regarding their origin; recent sociological research on the social effects of sex roles.

name: Topics in Sociology
description: Note: Consult the Department for any additional prerequisites. Course may be taken more than once if topic(s) vary.

name: Introduction to Social Methodology
description: Research design, data collection, and data processing strategies used by sociologists. Topics include research values and ethics, reliability and validity, experimentation, survey research techniques, historical methods, field research, and content analysis.

name: Youth, Crime and Society
description: A survey of the understanding and treatment of youth in the Canadian criminal justice system.

name: Sociology of Policing
description: Examines the nature of policing, its structure and function from a sociological perspective. Focus on theoretical analyses of policing, history of policing, police-citizen relations, strategies, powers, and culture of police, its public and private forms.

name: Topics in Criminology
description: Consult the Department for any additional prerequisites. Course may be taken more than once if topic(s) vary.

name: Criminal Justice Administration in Canada
description: The evolution and evaluation of the theories of punishment; the law, the police and the courts; penal and reformatory institutions; probation and parole; experiments in reform and rehabilitation.

name: Themes in Contemporary Social Theory
description: Major theoretical questions through analysis of works by contemporary theorists.  Not open to students with credit in SOC 332, 333, or 334.

name: Socialization
description: The processes of social development and how socio-cultural influences affect the individual from infancy to old age.  2889

name: Social Movements
description: How social movements arise and their impact on culture, society and politics.

name: Media Culture and Society
description: Critical examination of the central issues and debates about the media-society relationship. Emphasis on the cultural, political and economic aspects of various media forms/genres, media theories, and audience considerations. Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in SOC 346 and not to be use

name: Cultural Studies
description: Introduction to theoretical paradigms, methodologies and fundamental concepts of postmodern sociology and cultural studies.

name: Sociology of Work and Industry
description: Sociological analysis of the changing nature and content of work, its diversity of industrial contexts and organizational forms, and its consequences for individuals and society, from Canadian and comparative perspectives.  Not open to students with credit in SOC 366.

name: People in Industry
description: Introduction to the sociological analysis of the attitudes and behaviour of employees in work organizations, with emphasis on contemporary Canada. Note: Restricted to Engineering students only. Not open to students with credit in SOC 363.

name: Sociology of Globalization
description: Critically examines various aspects of globalization from the perspective of world-system studies.

name: Racism and Decolonization
description: Examines decolonizing cultural politics and theory with an emphasis on racism and its connection to other forms of social inequality and oppression.

name: Sociology of Aging
description: Aging as a socio-cultural phenomenon. Includes aging in relation to the self-concept, family, religion, politics, health, retirement and leisure, housing, attitudes toward death, with particular emphasis on Canadian society.

name: Sociology of Religion
description: Religion as a social phenomenon: theories of religious behaviour; religious authority and leadership; the individual's religion and the interplay with other spheres of social life; the role of religion in relation to social change and social integration.

name: Sociology of Youth
description: The comparative analysis of youth in various types of societies, with special emphasis on Canada including investigation of social structures and processes influencing behaviour of young people.

name: Sociology of Health and Illness
description: The distribution of health and illness in human populations, the social psychology of health and illness, and the social organization of health care.  2890

name: Field Placement in Criminology
description: Supervised work experience and seminar sessions. Note: Restricted to BA (Criminology) students.

name: Honors Individual Study
description: Individual study opportunity on topics for which no specific course is currently offered by the Department. Course may be taken once only.  Note: Restricted to Sociology Honors students. Closed to web registration.

name: Topics in Sociology
description: Note: Consult the Department for any additional prerequisites. Course may be taken more than once if topic(s) vary.

name: Individual Study
description: Individual study opportunity on topics for which no specific course is currently offered by the Department.

name: Honors Essay I
description: Literature review and proposal stage of Honors Essay completed in SOC 408.  Note: Restricted to Sociology Honors students. Closed to web registration.

name: Honors Essay II
description: Note: Restricted to Sociology Honors students. Closed to web registration.

name: Applied Research Internship
description: Supervised applied research experience and seminar sessions.

name: Qualitative Methods in Social Research
description: Further study of the design and evaluation of qualitative research strategies. Topics include participant observation, ethnomethodology, unobtrusive measures, and document analysis.

name: Topics in Criminal Justice
description: Note: Consult the Department for any additional prerequisites. Course may be taken more than once if topic(s) vary.

name: Sociology of Punishment
description: Historical and contemporary social underpinnings of punishment in the criminal justice apparatus.

name: Crime and Public Policy
description: Social responses to criminal behaviour, including general public attitudes and government legislation. Topics include police strategies; sentencing options; prediction research, and social prevention.

name: Sociology of Terrorism
description: A sociological engagement with the phenomenon of terrorism by state and non-state actors.  2891

name: Immigration, Ethnicity, and Crime
description: An exploration of the debates around immigration, ethnicity and crime.

name: Sociology of Law
description: Conceptual and practical points of convergence between legal and social theory; processes by which legal rules are created, maintained and changed; law as an instrument of social control and change.

name: Women and Crime
description: Key concepts, issues and debates with respect to women in the criminal justice system as offenders, defendants, prisoners, and victims.

name: Theorizing 'the Social': Critical Debates in Social Theory
description: Seminar in fundamental assumptions and philosophy of social sciences and the relation between explanatory and normative theorizing.

name: Sociology of Religious Sects
description: Examination of conversion, membership maintenance, member disaffiliation, and resource acquisition strategies among religious sects. Emphasis on Canadian examples.

name: Cities and Suburbia
description: The significance of urban social spaces and environments, architecture, planning, design and material culture.

name: Sociology of Human Fertility
description: Emphasis on the social, social-psychological, and cultural correlates of human fertility in historical and contemporary contexts; reproductive health programs; prediction and control.

name: Mortality and Population Health
description: Analysis of variations, trends and patterns of human mortality and morbidity in historical and contemporary contexts; comparisons of the experiences of Canada, other industrialized nations and developing countries with respect to causes of death and illness; demographic aspects of aging and its rela

name: Data Analysis and Research
description: A practical approach to social science research that focuses on methods for preparing, analyzing, and presenting data.

name: The Demography of Marriage and Family
description: Review and analysis of the demographic interrelationships of fertility, mortality, and migration with marriage and the family; a cross-cultural review of historical trends, contemporary patterns and future implications; emphasis on statistical measurement, family planning and policy in the Canadian

name: Sociology of Art
description: A sociological study of art forms including painting, literature, music, and architecture; cross-cultural analysis of the roles of the artist; the relationship of art forms and movements to different social conditions and social change.  2892

name: Sociology of Death and Dying
description: Comparative examination of death and dying in socio-cultural contexts, including theoretical and methodological issues.

name: Religion and Societies
description: A comparative survey of the major world religions in interaction with the socioeconomic and political structures of various societies.

name: Sociology of Mental Illness
description: Sociological aspects of mental health and illness. Includes historical perspectives, diagnostic issues, and perspectives on causation and treatment.

name: Queer-ing the Social
description: Sex/gender/sexuality as a complex social constellation.

name: Human Rights in International Perspective
description: Human rights theories, issues and controversies in local, national and international context.

name: Advanced Field Placement in Criminology
description: Supervised work experience and seminar sessions.  Note: Restricted to BA (Criminology) students. Sociology: Graduate Note: See also INT D 593 for a course which is offered by more than one Department or Faculty and which may be taken as an option or as a course in this discipline.

name: Conference Course in Sociology for Graduate Students
Conference Course in Sociology for Graduate Students
Quantitative Methods in Social Research
description: Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in SOC 412 or 417. Not available for credit for students with credit in R SOC 415.

name: Advanced Field Placement in Criminal Justice
description: Note: restricted to MA (Criminal Justice) students. 2893

Mortality and Population Health
description: Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in SOC 452.

name: Sociology of Human Fertility
description: Note: Not to be taken by students with credit in SOC 451.

name: Topics in Sociology
description: Course may be taken more than once if topics(s) vary.

Special Topics II
description: ★ 1.5 (fi 3) (either term, 0-1.5s-0)

name: Human Rights in Theory and Practice
description: An exploration of the sociology of human rights in theory and practice in a national and international context.

The Department reserves the right to place students in the language course appropriate to their level of language skill. 2. Placement tests may be administered in order to assess prior background. name: Beginners' Spanish I
description: Intended for students with no previous knowledge of the language and designed to lead to a working knowledge of spoken and written Spanish with a focus on listening, reading, speaking, writing, and intercultural competence. Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency, or

name: Beginners' Spanish II
description: A continuation of SPAN 111.  Note: not to be taken by students with native or near native proficiency, or with Spanish 30 or its equivalents in Canada and other countries.

name: Intermediate Spanish I
description: Intended to further develop knowledge of spoken and written Spanish with a focus on intercultural communicative competence.  Note: not to be taken by students with advanced standing equivalent or near native ability.

name: Intermediate Spanish II
description: A continuation of Spanish 211.  Note: Not to be taken by students with advanced standing equivalent or near native ability.

name: Advanced Spanish
description: A high-intermediate to advanced-level course intended to improve overall proficiency in spoken and written Spanish. Emphasis on intercultural communicative competence.  Note: Not to be taken by students with advanced standing equivalent or near native ability or with credit in SPAN 306.

name: Spanish for Heritage Speakers
description: Intended for speakers with an advanced level of oral proficiency, but no previous formal study of Spanish. Focus is on topics such as grammar and sentence structure, spelling and punctuation, interference between English and Spanish, and colloquial versus formal usages with the objective of improvin

name: Civilization and Culture of Spain
description: Through a series of selected topics, the course offers a panoramic view of Spanish civilization and culture from the Medieval Period to our present time with an overall emphasis on the construction of 'Spanish' cultural identities.

name: Introduction to Literary Analysis
description: Students develop the skills required for advanced studies in Spanish and Spanish American literature through the careful study of a variety of literary texts.

name: Foundational Fictions of Spanish America
description: Readings from selected texts to continue language acquisition and to introduce students to aspects of Spanish American literature and culture.

name: Latin American Literature and the Environment
description: The study of the relationship of Latin American writing and writers to the environment with a focus on the landscape and current ecological concerns.  2896

name: Introduction to Cinema
description: Some of the major works of film of Spain and/or Spanish America.

name: Hispanic Children's Literature
description: Key works of children's literature in Spain and Latin America.

name: The Culture of Food
description: Through an examination of texts about food (cookbooks, novels, films, music, journalism, etc.) students will gain an understanding of how food serves as a locus for concerns about nationalism, ethnicity, gender, and politics in Latin America.

name: Reading Popular Culture
description: The course will look at questions of nation, class, race and gender in Hispanic popular cultural production.

name: The Spanish Caribbean
description: Literature and culture in Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Dominican Republic.

name: The 'Roaring Twenties' in Transatlantic Perspective
description: Cultural production of Spanish America and Spain in the 1920's.

name: The Sounds of Spanish
description: Sound system of Spanish: phonetics, phonology, evolution of the language. Special attention to the pronunciation differences from English.

name: Spanish as a World Language
description: Focus on the evolution of Spanish from Latin and its spread around the world. Examines the different varieties of Spanish as well as practices and attitudes regarding the use of Spanish in different regions.  Note: Not open to students with credit in SPAN 372. May be taken in place of SPAN 372 for p

name: Teaching Spanish as a Second Language
description: Approaches to language teaching, Spanish grammar pedagogy, and specific issues in teaching Spanish structures and skills.  Note: Not open to students with credit in SPAN 478. May be taken in place of SPAN 478 for program requirements.

name: Exercises in Translation: Spanish into English
description: Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies. 2897

name: Exercises in Translation: English into Spanish
description: Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Advanced Grammar and Composition
description: Focus on meanings expressed by different grammatical structures in Spanish, with reference to translation differences with English. Practice of different styles of writing.  Note: This course can also be applied to the MLCS Certificate in Translation Studies.

name: Hispanic Filmmakers
description: Study of one to three different Hispanic filmmakers.

name: Creative Non-Fiction Workshop in Spanish
description: A writing workshop in Spanish. Students will gain exposure to evolving issues, standards, and research methodologies in professional writing. Genres may include literary journalism, memoir, travel writing, and personal essay.

name: Spanish Literary Masterpieces
description: An in-depth study of the most influential works of Span.

name: Reading Colonial Culture
description: Colonial Identities, power and ideology, mobilized through various forms of representation.

name: Self Portraits in Writing
description: Testimonial writing, biography, and autobiography, memories, correspondence, diaries, interviews and confessions.

name: Spanish in Society
description: Language as a social phenomenon. Description of dialects in Spanish. Language shift, bilingualism, language attrition, code- switching and langugage attitudes.

name: Exercises in Translation: Spanish to English
Exercises in Translation: English to Spanish

description: A writing workshop in Spanish. Students will gain exposure to evolving issues, standards, and research methodologies in professional writing. Genres may include literary journalism, memoir, travel writing, and personal essay.

name: Introduction to Applied Statistics I
description: Data collection and presentation, descriptive statistics. Probability distributions, sampling distributions and the central limit theorem. Point estimation and hypothesis testing. Correlation and regression analysis. Goodness of fit and contingency table.  Note : This course may not be taken for cre

description: ★ 3.8 (fi 6) (either term or Spring/Summer, 3-0-1.5) Descriptive data analysis. Calculus of Probability. Binomial, multinomial, Poisson, normal, beta, exponential, gamma, hypergeometric, and Weibull distributions. Sampling distributions. Estimation, testing hypotheses, goodness-of-fit tests, and one

name: Introduction to Applied Statistics II
description: Methods in applied statistics including regression techniques, analysis of variance and covariance, and methods of data analysis. Applications are taken from Biological, Physical and Social Sciences, and Business.  Notes: (1) Credit can be obtained in at most one of STAT 252, 319, 337 or 341. (2) Th

name: Statistics I
description: Sample space, events, combinatorial probability, conditional probability, independent events, Bayes Theorem, random variables, discrete random variables, expected values, moment generating function, inequalities, continuous distributions, multivariate distributions, independence.

name: Statistics II

name: Biostatistics
description: Methods of data analysis useful in Biostatistics including analysis of variance and covariance and nested designs, multiple regression, logistic regression and log-linear models. The concepts will be motivated by problems in the life sciences. Applications to real data will be emphasized through the

name: Life Contingencies I
description: Time at death random variables, continuous and discrete insurances, endowments and varying annuities, net premiums and reserves.

name: Sampling Techniques
description: Simple random sampling from finite populations, stratified sampling, regression estimators, cluster sampling.  Note: This course may only be offered in alternate years.

name: Introduction to Design and Analysis of Experiments
description: Basic principles of experimental design, completely randomized design-one way ANOVA and ANCOVA, randomized block design, Latin square design, Multiple comparisons. Nested designs. Factorial experiments.

name: Probability and Stochastic Processes
description: Problem solving of classical probability questions, random walk, gambler's ruin, Markov chains, branching processes. Selected topics of the instructor's choice.  2900

name: Mathematical Statistics
description: Laws of large numbers, weak convergence, some asymptotic results, delta method, maximum likelihood estimation, testing, UMP tests, LR tests, nonparametric methods (sign test, rank test), robustness, statistics and their sensitivity properties, prior and posterior distributions, Bayesian inference, c

name: Applied Regression Analysis
description: Simple linear regression analysis, inference on regression parameters, residual analysis, prediction intervals, weighted least squares. Multiple regression analysis, inference about regression parameters, multicollinearity and its effects, indicator variables, selection of independent variables. Non

name: Computational Statistics
description: Introduction to contemporary computational culture: reproducible coding, literate programming. Monte Carlo methods: random number generation, variance reduction, numerical integration, statistical simulations. Optimization: linear search, gradient descent, Newton-Raphson, and their specifics in the

name: Survival Analysis
description: Survival models, model estimation from complete and incomplete data samples, parametric survival models with concomitant variables, estimation of life tables from general population data.

name: Applied Statistical Methods for Data Mining
description: Principles of statistical model building and analysis applied in linear and generalized linear models and illustrated through multivariate methods such as repeated measures, principal components, and supervised and unsupervised classification.

name: Risk Theory
description: Classical ruin theory, individual risk models, collective risk models, models for loss severity: parametric models, tail behavior, models for loss frequency, mixed Poisson models; compound Poisson models, convolutions and recursive methods, probability and moment generating functions.

name: Probability I
description: Probability spaces, algebra of events. Elements of combinatorial analysis. Conditional probability, stochastic independence. Special discrete and continuous distributions. Random variables, moments, transformations. Basic limit theorems.

name: Time Series Analysis
description: Stationary series, spectral analysis, models in time series: autoregressive, moving average, ARMA and ARIMA. Smoothing series, computational techniques and computer packages for time series.  Note: This course may only be offered in alternate years.

name: Research Project
description: This course provides students in Specialization and Honors programs an opportunity to pursue research in statistics under the direction of a member of the Department. Course requirements include at least one oral presentation and a written final report. Students interested in taking this course shou

name: Directed Study I
description: Basic principles of experimental design, completely randomized design-one way ANOVA and ANCOVA. Randomized block design. Latin square design, Multiple comparisons. Nested designs. Factorial experiments. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods

name: Directed Study II
description: Simple linear regression analysis, inference on regression parameters, residual analysis, prediction intervals, weighted least squares. Multiple regression analysis, inference about regression parameters, multicollinearity and its effects, indicator variables, selection of independent variables. Non

name: Directed Study III
description: Theory and applications of time series modelling, stationarity, autocorrelation. Spectral properties, filtering. Box-Jenkins models, seasonality. Each student will give a written report and seminar presentation highlighting statistical methods used in a research project.

name: Directed Study IV
description: Basic sampling schemes for finite populations: simple random sampling, stratified random sampling, systematic sampling and cluster sampling. Unequal probability sampling. Ratio and regression estimators.  Note: Cannot be used for credit towards a graduate program in Statistics.

name: Directed Study V

name: Methods of Statistical Inference
description: An introduction to the theory of statistical inference. Topics to include exponential families and general linear models, likelihood, sufficiency, ancillarity, interval and point estimation, asymptotic approximations. Optional topics as time allows, may include Bayesian methods, Robustness, resampli

name: Design and Analysis of Experiments
description: The general linear model. Fully randomized designs, one-way layout, multiple comparisons. Block designs, Latin squares. Factorial designs confounding, fractions. Nested designs, randomization restrictions. Response surface methodology. Analysis of covariance.

name: Probability and Measure
description: Measure and integration, Laws of Large Numbers, convergence of probability measures. Conditional expectation as time permits.

name: Multivariate Analysis
description: The multivariate normal distribution, multivariate regression and analysis of variance, classification, canonical correlation, principal components, factor analysis.

name: Regression Analysis
description: Multiple linear regression, ordinary and generalized least squares, partial and multiple correlation. Regression diagnostics, collinearity, model building. Nonlinear regression. Selected topics: robust and nonparametric regression, measurement error models.

name: Stochastic Processes
description: Elements of stochastic processes. Discrete and continuous time Markov Chains; Birth and Death processes. Branching processes. Brownian Motion. General Stationary and Markov processes. Examples.  2903

name: Statistical Consulting
description: Data analysis, problem solving, oral communication with clients, issues in planning experiments and collecting data; practical aspects of consulting and report writing.

name: Reading in Statistics

name: Advanced Statistical Inference
description: Modern methods of statistical inference. Various versions of likelihood: conditional, marginal, integrated, profile, partial, empirical. Estimating equations. Semi-parametric models. Foundational issues.

name: Asymptotic Methods in Statistical Inference
description: Approximation techniques and asymptotic methods in statistics. Topics may include second and higher order expansions, asymptotics of likelihood based estimation and testing. Edgeworth expansions, exponential tilting, asymptotic relative efficiency, U-, M-, L-, and R-estimation.

name: Directed Research Project
description: Open only to students taking the MSc non-thesis option in statistics.

name: Behavior in Organizations
description: Provides an understanding of the behavior of individuals in organizations. Draws from psychology, sociology, organization theory and covers topics such as personality, motivation, leadership, communication, conflict, and group dynamics.  Open only to students from other faculties where the course is

name: Introduction to Management
description: Introduces students to the behavioral, political and organizational dynamics of managerial practice. Topics include management theory, social responsibility, ethics, motivation, decision making, leadership, organizational structure, and strategy. Not to be taken by students with credit in SMO 200 or

name: HRM: Managing the Work Force in Canada
description: This course is a general overview of human resource management issues in organizations. It focuses on reward systems, the design of work, legal issues, union-management relationships, staffing, and training and development.  Open to third- and fourth-year students.

name: Introduction to Strategic Management and Organization Design
description: Explores why organizations such as McDonalds, Northern Telecom, Bennetton, Wal-Mart and the University of Alberta use different patterns of organization. Examines the political and behavioral dynamics of management decision making.  Open to third- and fourth-year students.

name: Theory of Organizational Behaviour
description: Students who have taken introductory courses in the area will study in greater depth and detail theories of how people work in organizations. name: Introduction to Entrepreneurship
description: This is an interdisciplinary course for students interested in developing an idea for a new product or service into a market reality and an investable story. name: Management Skills for Supervisors and Leaders
description: The purpose of this course is to increase understanding of leadership roles and skill in exercising those roles. These include team building, mentoring, managing conflict, delegating, managing participative decision making, creative problem solving, and time and stress management.  Open to third- an

name: Interpersonal Communication and Team Management

description: Approaches to, and key debates about, sexuality. Topics may include: sexology; critiques of heterosexuality; political lesbianism; queer theory; transgender and intersexuality; prostitution and sex work. Note: Not open students with credit in W ST 370 or WGS 370

name: Indigenous Women, Autobiography, and Life Writing
description: Explores the ways in which Indigenous women have resisted colonial constructions of race and gender through autobiographical expressions and life writing. Includes study of memoirs, journals, confessions, diaries, personal essays, oral histories, and visual art. 2927

name: Critical Issues
description: This course offers an introduction to select issues in Women's, Gender, and Sexuality Studies. A variable content course, which may be repeated if topics vary.

name: History of Feminist Thought
description: Historical study of selected feminist writers and activists. Emphasis is on European and North American feminist thought up to the mid twentieth century.

name: Feminist Research and Methodologies
description: Exploration of feminist modes of research inquiry, feminist ethics in research, and critiques of traditional disciplinary approaches to research. Students will develop an understanding of theoretical issues involved in feminist research and will gain practical research skills.

name: Gender and Social Justice in Contemporary Africa
description: The lives of men and women in contemporary Africa, focusing on their experiences in the family, school, paid work, and the market, and on such development issues as health, environment, and human rights.

name: Feminism and Film
description: Feminist analysis of gender, sexuality, ability, race/ethnicity, and class/status in film. Content will vary in terms of genre, production, and language.

name: Contemporary Feminist Theory
description: The origins and evolution of various schools of contemporary western feminist thought. Not available to students with credit in PHIL 332.

name: Race, Class, and Gender
description: Historical, contemporary and comparative perspectives on the interaction of race, class, and gender experiences.

name: Gender, Conflict and Peace Building
description: The analysis of gender in international relations, drawing from literature on conflict and post conflict relations.

name: Canadian Feminist Activisms
description: An examination of contemporary feminist activisms with an emphasis on second- and third-wave feminisms. This course may be offered as a Community Service Learning course.

name: Environmental Feminisms and Social Justice
description: Addresses issues of environmental racism, sexism, and ableism, feminist approaches to environmental ethics, and social justice responses to climate change.

name: Directed Readings in Women's and Gender Studies
description: Open only to Women's and Gender Studies honors, majors and minors. Normally may be taken only once.  2928

name: Law and Feminism in Canada
description: A focus on the fundamentally contradictory role of law for women in Canada, building upon role of insights offered by feminist cross-disciplinary legal scholarship.

name: Feminism and Sexual Assault
description: Interdisciplinary consideration of conceptual, political and legal strategies that feminists have deployed to confront sexual coercion with an emphasis on contemporary North American context.

name: Body Politics
description: An examination of contemporary theoretical approaches to bodies and embodiment, with particular emphasis on the ways that race, class, sexuality, gender, and (dis)ability shape bodily experience.

name: Religion, Spirituality, and Social Justice
description: Ways in which adherents of world (Eastern and Western), indigenous, and neo-pagan religious systems have advocated for social justice. Note: Not open to students with credit in WGS 355 or W ST 355.

name: Masculinities
description: This course surveys the status of masculinity and the emergence of contemporary masculinity studies.

name: Sexualities: Special Topics
description: This course offers advanced examination of selected issues in sexuality studies.

name: Indigenous Feminisms
description: Draws on Indigenous theoretical frameworks, epistemologies, community expertise and knowledge to understand Indigenous women's participation in political movements and land and environmental activism. Students are strongly urged to complete *3 in NS before registering in WGS 4XX.

name: Topics in Women's and Gender Studies

description: A four-month work placement for Faculty of Science students admitted into the Science Internship program. The focus of the internship will be for students to perform work directly related to their degree. Work Experience registrations must be continuous.

name: Psychology Work Experience III

name: Introduction to Writing Poetry
description: Lectures and workshops in which the student will practice the art of poetry.

name: Introduction to Writing Fiction
description: Lectures and workshops in which the student will practice the craft of short prose fiction.

name: Introduction to Writing Nonfiction
description: To increase the student's ability to write clear nonfiction prose. Models of prose style are central, combined with frequent practice in writing on the basis of such models.  Not to be taken by students with credit in WRITE 298.

name: Introduction to Writing Nonfiction
description: To increase the student's ability to write clear nonfiction prose. Models of prose style are central, combined with frequent practice in writing on the basis of such models.  Not to be taken by students with credit in WRITE 297.

name: Intermediate Poetry
description: Lectures and workshops focusing on selected poetic technique and form.  Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Intermediate Fiction
description: Lectures and workshops focusing on selected fiction techniques and form.  Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Intermediate Creative Writing: Fiction
description: Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Intermediate Nonfiction
description: Lectures and workshop focusing on selected elements of nonfiction technique and form.  2933

name: Projects in Genre
description: Lectures and workshops emphasizing innovations across genres and/or specialized writing forms. Note: variable content course which may be repeated.  Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Advanced Creative Writing: Poetry
description: Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Advanced Creative Writing: Fiction
description: Consult Instructor for writing sample deadline.

name: Directed Creative Writing/Creative Research Project
description: ★ 3-6 (variable) (variable, variable) Design and completion of an undergraduate project under the guidance of a member of the Department. The project is to be an original creative project judged by the Department to be the equivalent of a half-year creative writing course for *3, or a full-year crea

name: Topics in Academic Writing
description: An introduction to the theory and practice of writing in various disciplines.

name: Exploring Writing
description: This workshop course focuses on both the theory and practice of the writing process to help students experience firsthand how university writers enter into rich ongoing conversations by engaging with the words and ideas of others.

name: Writing in the Disciplines
description: A blended learning course (combining online work and one weekly in-person meeting) that introduces students to academic writing in three broad areas: humanities, social sciences, and sciences. No prerequisite. May not be repeated. May contain alternative delivery sections; refer to the Fees Payment

name: Writing Persuasive Arguments
description: Introduction to the principles, theories and practice of writing well supported and convincing arguments.

name: Peer Tutor Training in Writing
description: Introduction to practices and strategies for tutoring undergraduate writers focusing on online and ESL writers.  2934

name: Introduction to Technical Writing
description: Analysis of and practice in key genres, processes, and strategies for technical communication.

name: Writing Special Effects: Rhetorical Grammar and Style
description: Analysis of and practice in sentence structures to create/alter meaning and voice in nonfiction prose.

name: Introduction to Professional Communication
description: Analysis of and practice in key genres, processes, and strategies for professional communication.

name: Introduction to Writing Centre Practice
description: Introduces students to the primary themes of interdisciplinary writing studies and collaborative learning necessary to successfully work as peer writing coaches in a university writing centre. Building on the foundation of theory, the course guides students through coaching sessions to synthesize an

name: Topics in Rhetoric
description: This lecture class introduces students to influential theories of rhetoric. Students will discuss and apply the theories in their analysis of texts and situations. Course may be repeated for credit if topics vary.  Not to be taken by students with credit in WRS 402.

name: Capstone Portfolio Project
description: Revision of documents and writing of new documents to create a portfolio of writing.  Writing Studies: Graduate

name: Academic Writing
description: This workshop course examines how to create persuasive, well-supported arguments in different genres in academic writing in all disciplines. Graduate students at all levels in all disciplines are welcome.

name: Writing Centre Theory
description: Introducing students to research in writing studies with a focus on writing in the disciplines and in writing centres.

name: Issues in Second Language Writing
description: 2935 Zoology (Biological Sciences): Undergraduate Department of Biological Sciences Faculty of Science Notes 1. See the following sections for listings of other Biological Sciences courses: Bioinformatics (BIOIN); Biology (BIOL); Botany (BOT); Entomology (ENT); Genetics (GENET); Microbiology (MICRB)

name: Vertebrate Diversity
description: A comparative survey of vertebrates, focusing on their morphology, classification, and phylogeny.

name: Animal Physiology I: Homeostasis
description: Survey of physiological systems that regulate levels of gases, food, energy, temperature, water, and ions. Examples from invertebrates and vertebrates. Students with credit in PHYSL 210 or 212 or 214 may not obtain credit in ZOOL 241.

name: Animal Physiology II: Intercellular Communication
description: Endocrinology, immunology and neural, sensory, motor, and reproductive physiology. Examples from invertebrates and vertebrates. Students with credit in PHYSL 210 or 212 or 214 may not obtain credit in ZOOL 242.

name: Survey of the Invertebrates
description: The functional anatomy and life cycles of the major invertebrate taxa are emphasized.

name: Animal Developmental Biology
description: An introduction to basic principles in animal development both in vertebrates and invertebrates. This course examines how the molecular, cellular and comparative approaches are integrated to explain the development of the egg into the embryo, and the cellular interactions that culminate in the devel

name: Comparative Anatomy of the Vertebrates
description: A comparative survey of form and function in vertebrate animals. Lectures focus on patterns of evolution and adaptation. Laboratories offer detailed examinations of major organ systems in representative species.  May not be taken for credit if credit already obtained in ZOOL 225.

name: Comparative Environmental Physiology
description: A comparative examination of the integrated responses of animals to environmental changes. This course focuses on both the acute physiological and long-term adaptations to dealing with environmental challenges. Focus is on biochemical and physiological responses to extreme environments.

name: Neurobiology
description: Nerve cells, nervous systems and neuromuscular systems from molecular, physiological, behavioral, and developmental perspectives. Examples from both invertebrates and vertebrates are given.  Students who have taken PMCOL 371 may not receive credit in ZOOL 342. 2936

name: Comparative Endocrinology
description: Endocrine systems and actions of hormones at the tissue, cell and molecular levels; vertebrate systems are emphasized.

name: Laboratory Exercises in Animal Physiology
description: Physiological topics are reinforced in experimental lab exercises. Labs include computer simulations, artificial tissue models and animal models.

name: Biology and Evolution of Invertebrates
description: Invertebrate evolution and adaptations including topics on feeding and nutrition, motility, reproduction and development, and sensory systems. Emphasis will be on material from the primary literature. Laboratory exercises will involve advanced training in techniques of microscopy.  ZOOL 303 and ZOOL

name: Freshwater Invertebrate Diversity
description: Emphasis is on an identified collection of invertebrates found in Alberta's lakes and streams. Lecture material pertains mainly to ecological features of the various fresh water groups.  Offered in alternate years.

name: Principles of Parasitism
description: An introduction to protozoan, helminth and arthropod parasites of animals; principles of host and parasite adaptations, host defense, pathology, epidemiology, and ecology, and control of parasitic infections. World wide web-based laboratory tutorials emphasize morphology, life cycles, behavior, syst

name: Wildlife Disease
description: Occurrence, principles, concepts, causes and significance of disease in wildlife. Laboratory exercises emphasize methods for the study of parasites of wild hosts.

name: Ethological Mechanisms
description: Animal behavior from an ethological perspective, with emphasis on the mechanisms underlying a variety of behaviors. The material is intended to complement that of ZOOL 371. Prerequisite or corequisite: ZOOL 241 or 242 or PHYSL 210, or 212 or 214. Offered in alternate years.

name: Behavioral Ecology
description: Animal behavior from an ecological and evolutionary perspective, with emphasis on social behavior. The material is intended to complement that of ZOOL 370.

name: Current Topics in Developmental Biology
description: Discussion of selected topics in animal developmental biology from a molecular and cellular perspective. Evaluation of the primary literature and communication skills are emphasized.  Credit for this course may be obtained more than once. Offered in alternate years.

name: Biology of Fishes
description: A survey of fish diversity focussing on the morphology, systematics, behavior, and ecology of the major groups. Laboratories feature extensive use of departmental collections, with an emphasis on Alberta species.  Offered in alternate years. 2937

name: Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles
description: A survey of amphibian and reptile diversity focussing on morphology, systematics, behaviour, and ecology of major groups, within a phylogenetic and evolutionary framework. Laboratories feature extensive use of departmental collections, and include Albertan species. This course will normally include

name: Biology of Birds
description: A survey of bird diversity focussing on the morphology, systematics, behaviour, and ecology of the major groups. Laboratories feature extensive use of departmental collections, with an emphasis on Alberta species.

name: Biology of Mammals
description: A survey of mammal diversity focussing on the morphology, systematics, behavior, and ecology of the major groups. Laboratories feature extensive use of departmental collections, with an emphasis on Alberta species.

name: Current Topics on Homeostasis
description: Discussion of selected topics in cardiac, gut, renal, respiratory, temperature, and metabolic physiology. Evaluation of the primary literature and communication skills are emphasized.  Credit for this course may be obtained more than once. Offered in alternate years.

name: Current Topics in Intercellular Communication
description: Discussion of selected topics in endocrinology, immunology, and neurobiology from molecular, cellular, and whole-animal perspectives. Evaluation of the primary literature and communication skills are emphasized.  Credit for this course may be obtained more than once. Offered in alternate years.

name: Topics in Parasitology
description: Experimental approaches to the study of parasitism, including topics on epidemiology, ecology, biochemistry, cell biology, genetics, molecular biology, pathology, and immunology of host-parasite relationships. Reading assignments emphasize experimental approaches to study eukaryotic host-parasite re

name: Advanced Current Topics in Developmental Biology
description: Discussion of selected topics in animal developmental biology from a molecular and cellular perspective. Evaluation of the primary literature and communication skills are emphasized.
